proposals.title: Defining the Goldilocks Zone: The Impact of Extracellular Matrix Scaffold Degradation Kinetics on Long-Term Functional Tissue Remodeling in a Porcine Volumetric Muscle Loss Model
proposals.motivation: Extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds are central to regenerative medicine, but their clinical translation is hindered by unpredictable outcomes. Pre-2026 work demonstrates that scaffold degradation releases bioactive matrix-bound nanovesicles and peptides that critically modulate the host immune response and cell behavior. However, the precise relationship between scaffold degradation rate and long-term functional tissue support remains undefined, posing a significant risk of complications from either chronic foreign body reactions (too slow) or premature structural collapse (too fast).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, intermediate degradation rate of an ECM scaffold exists that balances pro-regenerative immune modulation with sustained mechanical support, leading to superior functional tissue restoration, while deviations from this rate will result in complications such as fibrosis, inflammation, or hernia formation.
proposals.method: We will utilize a porcine volumetric muscle loss model. Urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogels will be chemically crosslinked (genipin, EDAC) or enzymatically pre-digested to create three distinct degradation kinetic profiles: slow (>6 months), intermediate (2-4 months), and fast (<1 month). Scaffolds will be implanted and animals monitored for 12 months. Outcomes will be assessed via longitudinal ultrasound for muscle thickness/contractility, endpoint histomorphometry (immunohistochemistry for M1/M2 macrophages, α-SMA, collagen I/III), biomechanical testing of repair site, and quantification of systemic and local ECM degradation products (MSD immunoassay).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, long-term functional data linking specific ECM scaffold degradation rates to defined clinical complications and regenerative outcomes. It will yield a predictive framework for tailoring scaffold degradation to specific clinical applications, moving the field from empirical design to rational engineering. The findings will directly inform the development of next-generation, rate-controlled ECM biomaterials.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies correlate degradation with short-term cellular responses, this work uniquely investigates the causal link between precisely engineered degradation kinetics and long-term *functional* tissue outcomes in a large, clinically relevant model, directly testing the assumption that any degradation is uniformly beneficial.

proposals.title: Long-Term Immunomodulatory Failure of ECM Scaffolds in a Rodent Model of Implant Capsule Formation: A Challenge to the Constructive Remodeling Paradigm
proposals.motivation: Capsular contracture remains a major complication of breast implants, driven by chronic inflammation and fibrosis. While extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds have shown promise in promoting constructive, site-appropriate tissue remodeling in other contexts, their long-term efficacy in preventing the fibrotic cascade around implants is unproven. This study addresses a critical gap by testing whether early constructive remodeling truly confers durable protection against late-stage contracture, a question with direct clinical implications for implant-based reconstruction and augmentation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while ECM hydrogel scaffolds will initially promote a pro-healing, M2-like macrophage response and reduce early fibrosis around implants, this immunomodulatory effect will not be sustained, ultimately failing to prevent the long-term myofibroblast activation and collagen contracture characteristic of capsular contracture.
proposals.method: We will use a well-established rodent model of submuscular silicone implant placement. The experimental group will receive a peri-implant injection of a decellularized, urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-derived hydrogel, based on Badylak's pre-2026 clinical work. Control groups will receive saline or a commercially available anti-adhesion barrier. Animals will be sacrificed at 30, 90, and 180-day endpoints. Capsules will be analyzed histologically (H&E, Masson's Trichrome, picrosirius red) for collagen density and organization, immunohistochemically for macrophage polarization (CD68, iNOS, CD206) and myofibroblast presence (α-SMA), and mechanically via uniaxial tensile testing to measure capsule stiffness. Luminex multiplex assays will quantify temporal cytokine profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first longitudinal, mechanistic evidence on the durability of ECM-mediated immunomodulation in the specific pro-fibrotic microenvironment of an implant. It will define whether early constructive remodeling is a reliable predictor of long-term success or a transient state, fundamentally informing the clinical translation of ECM-based strategies for contracture prevention.
proposals.novelty: This study directly challenges the assumed causal link between early constructive remodeling and permanent prevention of fibrosis, a novel concept in the field of biomaterial-tissue integration. It uniquely applies Badylak's established ECM immunomodulation principles to a long-term, dynamic model of a clinically persistent fibrotic condition.

proposals.title: Decade-Long Histologic and Immunomodulatory Assessment of Extracellular Matrix Scaffold Remodeling in a Porcine Model of Volumetric Muscle Loss
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 clinical use of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds demonstrates safety and efficacy in soft tissue reconstruction, but long-term fate (>5 years) remains poorly characterized. Understanding the final endpoint of scaffold degradation, the persistence of matrix-bound nanovesicles' immunomodulatory effects, and the quality of neotissue at a decade is critical for predicting lifelong performance and optimizing scaffold design for permanent integration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by 10 years post-implantation, ECM scaffolds will be fully degraded and replaced by site-appropriate, innervated, and vascularized functional tissue, with a resolved, pro-regenerative macrophage profile, and that the mechanical properties of the remodeled site will match native tissue.
proposals.method: We will conduct a long-term survival study using a established porcine model of volumetric muscle loss, implanting a clinical-grade urinary bladder matrix (UBM) scaffold. Animals will be serially sacrificed at 7, 8, 9, and 10+ years. Histology (H&E, Masson's Trichrome, Picrosirius Red) will quantify collagen architecture and scaffold remnants. Immunohistochemistry (CD206, CD86, CD3) will assess macrophage polarization and lymphocyte presence. Advanced imaging (second harmonic generation microscopy) will analyze collagen alignment, and biomechanical testing will evaluate tensile strength.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the ultralong-term remodeling trajectory of ECM scaffolds, defining the endpoint of the host-scaffold interaction. It will establish a critical safety and efficacy benchmark for regulatory science and inform the design of next-generation biomaterials intended for lifelong integration. The findings will directly impact clinical decision-making for permanent tissue replacement strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior Badylak group studies have tracked remodeling up to 63 months, this proposal extends the timeline to a full decade, a period previously unexplored in large animal models, to capture the final, stable state of the scaffold-tissue interface and its immunologic landscape.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Biomechanical Integration of Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds: A Tendon-Inspired Tensile Testing Framework for Predicting In Vivo Performance
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds depends on their ability to integrate with host tissue and provide mechanical support during remodeling. Current pre-2026 evaluations often rely on histology and molecular assays, lacking standardized, predictive biomechanical metrics for the strength of the scaffold-tissue composite. This gap hinders the rational design of scaffolds for load-bearing applications and the prediction of complications like anastomotic leaks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tensile testing methodologies standard in tendon biomechanics can quantify the mechanical integration of ECM scaffolds, and that specific mechanical parameters (e.g., ultimate tensile strength, modulus, failure mode) will correlate with established histological markers of constructive remodeling and predict functional outcomes in vivo.
proposals.method: We will adapt uniaxial tensile testing protocols from tendon/ligament research. Decellularized ECM scaffolds (e.g., urinary bladder matrix, small intestinal submucosa) will be implanted in a rodent abdominal wall defect model. At multiple time points, explants will be subjected to tensile testing to failure using a biomechanical tester, with digital image correlation for strain analysis. Mechanical data (stress-strain curves, UTS, stiffness) will be correlated with synchronous histological (H&E, picrosirius red) and immunohistochemical (e.g., macrophage phenotype) analyses from adjacent tissue sections.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a standardized biomechanical framework for evaluating ECM scaffold remodeling, moving beyond qualitative assessment. It will provide critical structure-function relationships linking scaffold mechanics to host response, offering a new predictive tool for scaffold design and pre-clinical evaluation. The findings could directly inform the development of next-generation ECM biomaterials for hernia repair, tendon reinforcement, and gastrointestinal anastomosis.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffold biology is well-studied, the direct application of rigorous soft tissue tensile testing to quantify the mechanical outcome of the host-scaffold integration process is novel. This approach transplants a mature engineering methodology from tendon research into the regenerative medicine evaluation pipeline, creating a new quantitative endpoint.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Control of Host Remodeling via an Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel Scaffold Co-Delivering Anti-Fibrotic and Pro-Angiogenic Cues
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds is often limited by unpredictable host remodeling, where excessive fibrosis can impede functional tissue regeneration and adequate vascularization. While ECM scaffolds provide a passive, inductive microenvironment, pre-2026 work demonstrates that scaffold degradation products possess inherent immunomodulatory and anti-neoplastic properties, suggesting an active signaling role. This creates an opportunity to actively program the scaffold to steer the host response towards a more regenerative, rather than fibrotic, outcome.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the controlled, sequential release of an anti-fibrotic agent (e.g., pirfenidone) and a pro-angiogenic factor (e.g., VEGF) from a urinary bladder matrix (UBM) hydrogel will synergistically modulate the innate immune response and stromal cell activity to suppress pathological fibrosis and promote functional vascularization in a rodent model of volumetric muscle loss.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a UBM hydrogel and functionalize it using a layer-by-layer electrostatic binding technique to create a dual-release system. The fast-release anti-fibrotic agent will be incorporated into the hydrogel bulk, while the sustained-release pro-angiogenic factor will be encapsulated in poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) microparticles embedded within the matrix. The release kinetics will be characterized in vitro. The functionalized scaffold will be implanted in a rat abdominal wall defect model. Outcomes will be assessed at 7, 14, and 28 days via histomorphometry (collagen deposition, vessel density), multiplex immunofluorescence (macrophage polarization: CD68, iNOS, CD206), and functional biomechanical testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for an 'active' ECM scaffold that goes beyond passive support to therapeutically direct the host response. It will provide critical data on the feasibility of spatiotemporal drug delivery from decellularized matrices and generate new knowledge on how combined immunomodulatory and angiogenic cues can be orchestrated to improve functional tissue restoration.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds have been used as passive drug carriers, the novelty lies in the intentional design for sequential, multi-agent release targeting specific, antagonistic pathways of the healing response (fibrosis vs. angiogenesis) to proactively shape the remodeling landscape, leveraging the scaffold's inherent bioactivity as a foundation.

proposals.title: Computational Design and In Vivo Validation of a Porosity-Gradient Extracellular Matrix Scaffold to Accelerate Constructive Remodeling in Volumetric Muscle Loss
proposals.motivation: The constructive remodeling of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds is a critical determinant of functional tissue restoration, yet the process is often slow and variable. While ECM scaffolds are known to modulate the host immune response towards a pro-regenerative phenotype, the specific biophysical and biochemical cues that optimally accelerate this transition remain poorly defined. An inverse design approach, starting from a desired remodeling outcome, could systematically identify scaffold parameters that enhance the rate and quality of tissue repair.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an ECM scaffold engineered with a specific, spatially defined gradient in porosity and crosslinking density will accelerate the transition from the inflammatory to the pro-regenerative phase of the host response, leading to faster constructive remodeling and functional muscle recovery in a rodent model of volumetric muscle loss.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational inverse design framework using a genetic algorithm to optimize a 3D scaffold architecture. The algorithm will be trained on a finite element model that simulates cell infiltration, macrophage polarization (M1 to M2), and neotissue deposition as functions of local porosity, pore interconnectivity, and matrix-bound nanovesicle retention. The top-performing virtual design will be fabricated from decellularized porcine urinary bladder ECM using a combination of controlled freeze-drying and carbodiimide crosslinking to create the prescribed porosity gradient. The scaffold will be evaluated in a rat VML model, with outcomes assessed via longitudinal histology (H&E, immunofluorescence for macrophage phenotypes), biomechanical testing, and functional gait analysis at 2, 4, and 8 weeks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, causal link between engineered scaffold biophysical properties and the kinetics of the pro-healing immune response in vivo. It will yield a novel, rationally designed ECM scaffold with demonstrated efficacy for accelerating muscle repair. Furthermore, it will provide a generalizable computational-experimental pipeline for the inverse design of biomaterials for other regenerative applications.
proposals.novelty: This proposal moves beyond the empirical, trial-and-error modification of scaffolds to a predictive, outcome-driven design paradigm. It uniquely integrates computational modeling of the host immune response with precise scaffold fabrication to test a spatially complex architectural cue—a porosity gradient—explicitly intended to stage and accelerate the temporal sequence of remodeling.

proposals.title: Source-Dependent Immunomodulatory Cargo and Function of Matrix-Bound Nanovesicles from Different Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds
proposals.motivation: Matrix-bound nanovesicles (MBVs) are recognized as key bioactive components of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds, driving immunomodulatory and pro-healing effects. A prevailing assumption is that MBVs from all decellularized ECM sources possess a conserved, universally beneficial cargo. This assumption, if incorrect, could limit the rational design of ECM-based therapeutics by overlooking source-specific bioactivity, potentially explaining variable clinical outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: The protein, lipid, and miRNA cargo of MBVs, and consequently their immunomodulatory effects on macrophages, are not identical but are significantly influenced by the tissue source (e.g., small intestinal submucosa, urinary bladder, dermis) and species of origin of the parent ECM scaffold.
proposals.method: MBVs will be isolated via established differential centrifugation from decellularized ECM scaffolds derived from porcine small intestinal submucosa (SIS), urinary bladder matrix (UBM), and dermis. Cargo will be profiled using mass spectrometry (proteomics, lipidomics) and small RNA sequencing (miRNA). Functional assays will quantify the polarization of primary human macrophages (M0 to M1/M2 phenotypes) via flow cytometry (CD80, CD206) and cytokine multiplex analysis (TNF-α, IL-10, IL-1ra) following MBV exposure. Statistical analysis will employ principal component analysis and ANOVA to identify source-dependent clusters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, multi-omic comparison of MBV cargo across clinically relevant ECM sources, directly testing the assumption of functional equivalence. It will establish a foundational dataset linking specific ECM source to MBV bioactivity, enabling the future selection or engineering of ECM scaffolds for targeted immunomodulation in specific clinical applications.
proposals.novelty: While MBVs are known to be bioactive, the explicit challenge that their cargo and effects are source-independent is novel. Prior work often treats ECM scaffolds as a monolithic source; this proposal posits that the 'tissue memory' encoded in MBVs is source-specific, a nuanced distinction with significant translational implications.

proposals.title: Characterization of the Pro-Regenerative Phenotype of Mesenchymal Stromal Cells Isolated from Pathological Cyst Environments
proposals.motivation: Pathological cysts are clinically managed as biological waste, yet their fluid-filled lumen and surrounding capsule constitute a unique, dynamic, and chronically inflamed tissue microenvironment. Pre-2026 work by Badylak and others has established that the extracellular matrix (ECM) and its degradation products in pathological settings can profoundly influence cell behavior and immunomodulation. Investigating whether this environment endows resident or recruited cells with specialized functions could transform a clinical burden into a novel source of therapeutic cells.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) isolated from pathological cyst environments (e.g., pancreatic pseudocysts, renal cysts) will exhibit a distinct, pro-regenerative phenotype characterized by enhanced immunomodulatory capacity and trophic factor secretion compared to MSCs from conventional sources like bone marrow or adipose tissue.
proposals.method: Cyst-derived MSCs (C-MSCs) will be isolated from surgically resected human cyst wall tissues (IRB-approved) and characterized via flow cytometry for standard MSC markers. Their functional phenotype will be assessed through in vitro co-culture assays with peripheral blood mononuclear cells to quantify immunomodulation (T-cell proliferation, macrophage polarization to M2). Secretome analysis will be performed via multiplex cytokine arrays and proteomic profiling of conditioned media. The bioactivity of the C-MSC secretome and its interaction with cyst-derived ECM degradation products will be tested in a rodent model of dermal wound healing, comparing outcomes to those mediated by standard MSCs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the foundational phenotype and therapeutic potential of C-MSCs, directly testing the principle that pathological microenvironments can be a source of uniquely potent regenerative cells. It will provide a novel cellular resource for regenerative medicine and offer a new paradigm for viewing certain pathological structures not merely as disease byproducts but as potential reservoirs of therapeutic agents.
proposals.novelty: While the immunomodulatory properties of MSCs from bone marrow or fat are well-studied, the systematic isolation and characterization of MSCs from the chronic, fluid-filled niche of a pathological cyst is entirely novel. This inverts the traditional view of cysts, seeking therapeutic value from their intrinsic biology rather than focusing solely on their removal.

proposals.title: A Decellularized Extracellular Matrix-Derived Hydrogel Scaffold for the Controlled Delivery and Pro-Regenerative Polarization of Human Pericyte-Derived Mesenchymal Stem Cells
proposals.motivation: Human pericyte-derived mesenchymal stem cells (hPCy-MSCs) are a promising, readily accessible cell source for regenerative therapies due to their multipotency and perivascular niche. However, effective clinical translation requires a delivery vehicle that supports cell viability, guides differentiation, and modulates the host immune response towards a pro-regenerative, anti-fibrotic phenotype. Pre-2026 work by Badylak's group has demonstrated the unique immunomodulatory and pro-healing properties of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds and their degradation products, but a scaffold specifically engineered to leverage these properties for hPCy-MSC delivery is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a three-dimensional, porous hydrogel scaffold derived from urinary bladder matrix (UBM-ECM) will provide a conducive microenvironment for hPCy-MSC adhesion, proliferation, and pro-regenerative secretory activity, and that the combined therapy will synergistically enhance tissue repair by promoting a constructive macrophage polarization (M2-like phenotype) and reducing fibrotic scarring in vivo.
proposals.method: We will fabricate 3D scaffolds by solubilizing porcine UBM-ECM and inducing hydrogel formation via pH and temperature control, optimizing porosity and stiffness for cell infiltration. hPCy-MSCs will be isolated from human adipose tissue, characterized, and seeded onto the scaffolds. In vitro, we will assess cell viability, morphology, and secretome (ELISA for VEGF, HGF, PGE2). The immunomodulatory capacity will be tested using a macrophage co-culture assay, quantifying M1/M2 markers (iNOS, CD206) via flow cytometry. In vivo efficacy will be evaluated in a rat model of volumetric muscle loss, comparing ECM scaffold + hPCy-MSCs to cells or scaffold alone, measuring functional recovery, histology for myofiber regeneration, and immune cell infiltration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, clinically relevant cell-scaffold construct for soft tissue regeneration. It will provide mechanistic insight into how ECM scaffolds direct the therapeutic function of a specific MSC population. The findings will advance the field of regenerative medicine by demonstrating a synergistic, off-the-shelf compatible strategy that leverages both matrix biology and cellular therapy without requiring long-term cell engraftment.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds and MSCs have been studied separately, this proposal uniquely focuses on hPCy-MSCs—a developmentally relevant perivascular progenitor—delivered via a scaffold specifically designed to present bioactive matrix-bound nanovesicles (MBVs) and other cryptic peptides known from pre-2026 research to promote healing without immunosuppression.

proposals.title: Evaluation of Human Pericyte-Derived Mesenchymal Stromal Cells (hPCy-MSCs) Delivered via Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel for Enhanced Bone Regeneration in a Critical-Sized Calvarial Defect Model
proposals.motivation: Critical-sized bone defects remain a significant clinical challenge, often requiring complex grafting procedures with associated morbidity and limited integration. Pre-2026 research highlights the immunomodulatory and pro-regenerative potential of mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) and the unique ability of extracellular matrix (ECM)-based scaffolds to provide a supportive, bioactive microenvironment. Combining a novel, tissue-specific MSC source (pericytes) with a proven ECM hydrogel delivery system offers a promising strategy to overcome limitations in cell survival, retention, and directed differentiation at the injury site.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hPCy-MSCs, when delivered in a porcine urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogel, will exhibit superior survival, engraftment, and osteogenic differentiation compared to bone marrow-derived MSCs (BM-MSCs), leading to accelerated and more complete healing of critical-sized calvarial defects in a rodent model.
proposals.method: hPCy-MSCs will be isolated and characterized from human adipose tissue microvessels. A critical-sized (5mm) calvarial defect will be created in immunocompromised rats. Experimental groups will be: 1) UBM-ECM hydrogel alone, 2) UBM-ECM hydrogel + BM-MSCs (clinical standard control), and 3) UBM-ECM hydrogel + hPCy-MSCs. Cells will be pre-labeled for tracking. Healing will be assessed longitudinally over 12 weeks using micro-CT for bone volume/tissue mineral density, histology (H&E, Masson's Trichrome) for tissue morphology and collagen deposition, and immunohistochemistry for osteocalcin/osteopontin and human nuclear antigen to track donor cells. Systemic and local immune responses will be profiled via multiplex cytokine analysis of serum and defect lysates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first in vivo efficacy data for hPCy-MSCs in bone regeneration, directly comparing them to the clinical standard (BM-MSCs). It will establish the UBM-ECM hydrogel as an optimized delivery vehicle for perivascular stem cells. The findings will contribute a foundational preclinical dataset supporting the translational potential of a combined cell-matrix therapeutic for orthopedic and craniofacial reconstruction.
proposals.novelty: While ECM hydrogels and MSCs have been studied separately, the use of pericyte-derived MSCs—a progenitor cell population intimately associated with vasculature and native tissue microenvironments—delivered via a bioactive ECM hydrogel is a novel combinatorial approach. This leverages Badylak lab's expertise in ECM biology to potentially enhance the therapeutic action of a distinct, potent cell source.

proposals.title: Standardized Isolation and Expansion of Human Pancreatic Cyst-Derived Mesenchymal Stromal Cells (hPCy-MSCs) Using Decellularized Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds
proposals.motivation: Human pancreatic cyst-derived MSCs (hPCy-MSCs) represent a promising, tissue-specific cell source for regenerative therapies, particularly for pancreatic and gastrointestinal applications. However, the lack of standardized, reproducible protocols for their isolation and expansion from heterogeneous cyst tissue samples hinders comparative studies and clinical translation. Establishing a robust methodology is critical to harness their unique immunomodulatory and pro-regenerative potential, as demonstrated by Dr. Badylak's prior work on ECM-derived therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a standardized protocol utilizing enzymatic digestion followed by culture on tissue-specific, decellularized pancreatic extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds will yield hPCy-MSCs with higher purity, viability, and consistent functional phenotypes (e.g., immunomodulatory secretome) compared to conventional plastic culture.
proposals.method: Cyst tissue samples will be processed using a systematic comparison of enzymatic digestion parameters (collagenase type, concentration, time). Isolated cells will be expanded under two conditions: standard tissue culture plastic and coatings derived from decellularized porcine pancreatic ECM. We will employ fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) using a defined panel of surface markers (CD73+, CD90+, CD105+, CD45-, CD34-) to assess purity. Functional assays will include proliferation kinetics, trilineage differentiation potential, and quantification of immunomodulatory factors (PGE2, IL-10, TGF-β) in conditioned media.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first community-adoptable, step-by-step protocol for reliable hPCy-MSC isolation, directly addressing a key bottleneck in the field. It will establish critical quality control benchmarks for phenotype and function. Furthermore, it will generate preliminary data on the utility of ECM scaffolds for enhancing the yield and therapeutic potency of these cells for future clinical applications.
proposals.novelty: While MSC isolation from various tissues is common, the specific application to pancreatic cyst tissue and the integration of decellularized ECM as a culture substrate to mimic a native niche for expansion is novel. This approach directly leverages Dr. Badylak's expertise in ECM biology to solve a cell sourcing problem.

proposals.title: A Decellularized Extracellular Matrix-Integrated Organ-on-a-Chip Platform to Model hPCy-MSC Mediated Tissue Regeneration
proposals.motivation: Human perivascular stem cells (hPCy-MSCs) are a promising, translationally relevant cell source for regenerative medicine, but their therapeutic mechanisms within a dynamic, tissue-specific microenvironment remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 organ-on-a-chip technology offers unprecedented control over biomechanical and biochemical cues, yet its integration with clinically relevant biologic scaffolds, like decellularized extracellular matrix (ECM), is limited. This gap hinders the development of predictive human models for testing ECM scaffold-cell interactions critical for clinical success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that culturing hPCy-MSCs within a microfluidic organ chip lined with tissue-specific decellularized ECM will recapitulate key aspects of in vivo regeneration—including stem cell differentiation, paracrine signaling, and immunomodulation—more accurately than static 2D or 3D culture systems.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a dual-channel, membrane-based polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic chip. The central cell culture channel will be coated with a thin hydrogel derived from urinary bladder matrix (UBM-ECM), a well-characterized scaffold in Badylak's work. hPCy-MSCs will be seeded into this ECM-lined channel under continuous, physiologically relevant perfusion. A parallel vascular channel will be lined with endothelial cells to model perfusion and barrier function. We will apply cyclic strain to simulate tissue-specific mechanical forces (e.g., peristalsis for esophageal or intestinal models). Cell phenotype (flow cytometry for CD34, CD146), matrix deposition/degradation (mass spectrometry for ECM degradation products), and secretory profile (multiplex cytokine array) will be analyzed.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, physiologically relevant testbed to dissect the pro-regenerative mechanisms of hPCy-MSCs within a defined ECM microenvironment, bridging in vitro models and in vivo outcomes. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on how ECM composition and dynamic cues direct stem cell function, directly informing the design of next-generation ECM-based clinical scaffolds. The platform could reduce animal use in testing regenerative therapies.
proposals.novelty: While organ chips and ECM scaffolds exist separately, their integration to study a clinically relevant adult stem cell population (hPCy-MSCs) is novel. The focus on modeling the dynamic interplay between ECM degradation products, mechanical cues, and stem cell immunomodulation—a core theme in Badylak's recent work—within a controlled microfluidic system represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Injectable, Thermo-Responsive ECM Hydrogel for the Minimally Invasive Treatment of Complex Perianal Fistulas
proposals.motivation: Complex perianal fistulas are debilitating conditions with high recurrence rates post-surgery. Current ECM scaffold implants require invasive surgical placement, which is challenging in the irregular, infected tracts of fistulas and carries risks of failure and patient morbidity. A minimally invasive delivery method could revolutionize treatment by enabling precise filling of complex tracts without major surgery, aligning with the broader shift towards less invasive therapeutic interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: An injectable, thermo-responsive hydrogel derived from decellularized porcine urinary bladder ECM (UBM) will undergo sol-gel transition at body temperature to form a pro-regenerative matrix in situ, effectively promoting fistula tract closure and functional tissue restoration without surgical scaffold implantation.
proposals.method: We will produce UBM powder and solubilize it via pepsin digestion to create a liquid precursor. Rheological characterization will optimize polymer concentration and salt content to achieve injectability at room temperature and rapid gelation at 37°C. The hydrogel's ability to support macrophage polarization toward an M2 pro-healing phenotype will be assessed in vitro. Efficacy will be tested in a rat model of surgically created colon-cutaneous fistula, comparing hydrogel injection to surgical UBM scaffold implantation and controls, with outcomes measured by tract closure, histology (immune cell profiling, collagen organization), and mechanical integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational platform for injectable ECM therapies, moving the field beyond implantable scaffolds. It will provide proof-of-concept for treating a surgically challenging condition with a minimally invasive, immunomodulatory matrix. The data will critically inform clinical translation pathways for ECM-derived injectables.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the core paradigm of ECM-based therapies from a surgically placed, structural 'scaffold' to an injectable, in-situ assembling 'matrix instruction,' leveraging ECM's bioactive properties without the constraints of pre-formed implant geometry and surgical trauma.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Efficacy of an Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel for Anastomotic Healing in a Diabetic Rat Model of Colorectal Surgery
proposals.motivation: Preclinical testing of biomaterials like extracellular matrix (ECM) hydrogels often uses healthy, young animal models, which poorly represent the compromised healing environment of typical surgical patients. Comorbidities like diabetes mellitus are prevalent and significantly impair wound healing through dysregulated inflammation and angiogenesis. A rigorous evaluation of ECM-based therapies in a clinically relevant, compromised model is therefore essential prior to clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that local application of a urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogel will significantly improve anastomotic healing metrics—including burst pressure, collagen deposition, and pro-regenerative macrophage polarization—in a streptozotocin-induced diabetic rat model, compared to untreated diabetic controls and approaching outcomes in healthy animals.
proposals.method: We will induce type 1 diabetes in Sprague-Dawley rats using streptozotocin and confirm hyperglycemia. A standardized rectal anastomosis model will be performed. Rats will be randomized into three groups: diabetic + UBM-ECM hydrogel, diabetic + saline control, and non-diabetic + saline control. The hydrogel will be applied perianastomotically at surgery. Primary outcomes will be anastomotic leak rate and burst pressure at 7 days. Secondary analyses will include histology (H&E, Masson's trichrome), immunohistochemistry for macrophages (CD68, iNOS, CD206), and quantification of key cytokines (e.g., VEGF, TGF-β1) in the peri-anastomotic tissue.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical pre-clinical data on the performance of an ECM hydrogel in a high-risk healing environment, directly informing patient selection for future clinical trials. It will advance the field of regenerative medicine by defining the immunomodulatory and pro-healing effects of matrix-bound nanovesicles and ECM degradation products in a model of dysregulated metabolism.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Badylak's group has shown UBM-ECM hydrogel efficacy in healthy rodent anastomosis models, its systematic evaluation in a standardized diabetic model—a major surgical comorbidity—is novel. This directly challenges the sufficiency of healthy-animal data for predicting clinical success.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Interrogation of Early Intestinal Healing Using Organoid-Fibroblast Co-cultures on a Decellularized Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel Platform
proposals.motivation: Anastomotic leaks following gastrointestinal surgery remain a major clinical challenge, contributing to significant morbidity and mortality. Pre-2026 work from the Badylak lab demonstrated that extracellular matrix (ECM) hydrogels, like urinary bladder matrix (UBM), promote healing and reduce leaks in rodent models, but the precise cellular and molecular mechanisms driving this pro-healing microenvironment are incompletely understood. A reductionist, human-relevant in vitro model is needed to dissect the critical early interactions between epithelial cells and stromal fibroblasts within an ECM scaffold.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that UBM hydrogel will orchestrate a pro-regenerative crosstalk between intestinal epithelial organoids and fibroblasts in co-culture, recapitulating key early healing events—specifically, enhanced epithelial proliferation/migration and an immunomodulatory fibroblast phenotype—through the presentation of matrix-bound nanovesicles and bioactive cryptic peptides.
proposals.method: Human intestinal organoids will be derived from patient-derived biopsies or established cell lines and co-cultured with primary human intestinal fibroblasts within a 3D UBM hydrogel scaffold, as previously optimized by the Badylak lab. Dynamic interactions will be monitored via live-cell imaging for epithelial closure in scratch assays and organoid expansion. Mechanistic insight will be gained by profiling fibroblast secretome (multiplex cytokine arrays) and epithelial transcriptome (RNA-seq) in response to UBM, with key pathways validated using neutralizing antibodies against candidate ECM-derived factors (e.g., TGF-β, VEGF) and matrix-bound nanovesicle depletion controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, physiologically relevant human in vitro model for studying intestinal repair. It will directly link the known clinical benefits of UBM hydrogel to specific cellular behaviors and paracrine signaling events, identifying key effector molecules. The findings will provide a mechanistic foundation for optimizing ECM-based biomaterials and identifying therapeutic targets to prevent anastomotic failure.
proposals.novelty: While organoid and ECM hydrogel models exist separately, their integration with stromal co-culture specifically to deconstruct the mechanism of ECM-driven healing is novel. This approach moves beyond observational animal studies to actively test how UBM's structural and bioactive components instruct a pro-healing cellular dialogue.

proposals.title: Topical Application of an Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel for Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Delivery Enhances Wound Healing Efficacy Compared to Intradermal Injection in a Diabetic Murine Model
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) therapy for chronic wounds is promising, but optimal delivery remains unclear. Pre-2026 work by Badylak and others demonstrates that extracellular matrix (ECM) hydrogels provide a pro-regenerative microenvironment, yet the field often assumes that scaffold-based delivery via injection is superior for cell retention and function. This assumption overlooks the potential benefits of a simpler, non-invasive topical application, which may better mimic the natural wound environment and modulate the host immune response more effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that delivering MSCs within a topical ECM hydrogel will result in superior wound healing outcomes—characterized by enhanced re-epithelialization, angiogenesis, and pro-healing macrophage polarization—compared to delivering the same cells via intradermal injection in an ECM suspension, by providing sustained, localized immunomodulatory cues.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a decellularized urinary bladder matrix (UBM) hydrogel, as previously established in the Badylak lab. Human bone marrow-derived MSCs will be suspended in the UBM hydrogel for topical application or in saline for intradermal injection around full-thickness wounds in diabetic (db/db) mice. Wounds will be assessed daily for closure rate. Histological (H&E, Masson's trichrome) and immunohistochemical (CD31 for vessels, CD206/CD86 for macrophages) analyses at days 7 and 14 will quantify angiogenesis and immune cell profiles. In vitro, we will use transwell migration and proliferation assays with fibroblasts and endothelial cells exposed to conditioned media from both delivery methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly compare two clinically relevant delivery strategies for MSC-based wound therapy, providing evidence to challenge the default preference for injection. It will elucidate the mechanism by which topical ECM hydrogel application may superiorly modulate the wound immune microenvironment. The findings could shift clinical practice towards less invasive, more effective cell delivery for chronic wounds.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the prevailing scaffold-delivery paradigm by positing that the mode of application (topical vs. injected) of an ECM scaffold is a critical variable, not just its presence. It uniquely integrates Badylak's expertise in ECM hydrogels and immunomodulation to test a counter-intuitive, simplified delivery method for cell therapy.

proposals.title: Extracellular Matrix Scaffold-Mediated Immunomodulation by Mesenchymal Stromal Cells: A Paracrine Mechanism for Wound Healing Independent of Direct Differentiation
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) are widely used in regenerative medicine, but their therapeutic mechanism remains debated. The prevailing paradigm that they engraft and directly differentiate into tissue-specific cells is increasingly challenged by evidence of poor long-term engraftment, suggesting their primary role may be paracrine. Given Dr. Badylak's pre-2026 work demonstrating the profound immunomodulatory effects of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds and matrix-bound nanovesicles, it is critical to dissect whether ECM scaffolds act as a passive delivery vehicle for MSCs or actively synergize with their paracrine functions to orchestrate healing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary therapeutic mechanism of MSCs delivered via ECM scaffolds in a full-thickness wound model is paracrine immunomodulation, specifically the polarization of macrophages to a pro-regenerative M2 phenotype, and that this effect is independent of significant MSC differentiation or long-term engraftment.
proposals.method: We will utilize a rat full-thickness abdominal wall defect model. Human bone marrow-derived MSCs will be seeded onto a porcine urinary bladder ECM scaffold. Experimental groups will include: ECM scaffold alone, ECM+MSCs (live), and ECM+MSCs (paracrine-only, using a transwell system or conditioned medium). Healing will be assessed at 7, 14, and 28 days. Key analyses will include: 1) In vivo tracking of MSCs via luciferase imaging and immunohistochemistry for human-specific markers to quantify engraftment, 2) Multiplex cytokine profiling of wound fluid, 3) Flow cytometry and immunofluorescence for macrophage phenotypes (M1: iNOS+, M2: CD206+), and 4) Histomorphometry for angiogenesis and collagen deposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a fundamental mechanistic assumption in MSC-based therapies, providing definitive evidence for or against the differentiation paradigm in a clinically relevant scaffold context. It will elucidate the synergistic role of ECM scaffolds in modulating the host immune response via delivered MSCs, informing the design of more effective, cell-free therapeutic strategies that mimic this paracrine activity.
proposals.novelty: While paracrine effects of MSCs are recognized, this study uniquely integrates them with the specific immunomodulatory properties of a clinically relevant ECM scaffold, moving beyond correlation to directly test causality by isolating the paracrine component and measuring its sufficiency for healing in vivo.

proposals.title: An Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel Scaffold Enables Allogeneic Mesenchymal Stromal Cell-Mediated Wound Healing Without Systemic Immunosuppression in an Immunocompetent Rodent Model
proposals.motivation: Allogeneic mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) offer an 'off-the-shelf' therapy for complex wounds but are typically administered with systemic immunosuppression to prevent rejection, which increases infection risk and complicates clinical translation. Pre-2026 work by Badylak's group has demonstrated that extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds and their derivatives, such as matrix-bound nanovesicles (MBVs), actively modulate the local immune microenvironment towards a pro-regenerative, anti-inflammatory state. This presents a unique opportunity to leverage ECM biomaterials to locally protect allogeneic cells, potentially eliminating the need for harmful systemic drugs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local immunomodulatory properties of a decellularized ECM hydrogel scaffold will create a privileged microenvironment that allows allogeneic MSCs to survive, engraft, and promote healing in a full-thickness cutaneous wound without concurrent systemic immunosuppression.
proposals.method: We will use a rat full-thickness excisional wound model. Syngeneic or allogeneic MSCs will be delivered in a saline suspension (control) or suspended within a urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogel, a well-characterized scaffold from Badylak's lab. A positive control group will receive allogeneic MSCs with systemic tacrolimus. Wound closure kinetics will be tracked. At key timepoints, wounds will be harvested for histology (H&E, immunofluorescence for MSC markers, T-cell and macrophage subsets) and multiplex cytokine analysis of wound fluid to characterize the local immune response and MSC persistence.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test a clinically transformative paradigm: using biomaterial design, not pharmacology, to solve the immunogenicity of allogeneic cell therapies. It will provide mechanistic insight into how ECM scaffolds interact with donor cells and the host immune system. A positive result would significantly advance the translational pathway for off-the-shelf regenerative therapies.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are known to be immunomodulatory, their specific capacity to shield allogeneic therapeutic cells from adaptive immune rejection in an immunocompetent wound healing context remains an untested and non-obvious application, moving beyond their use as passive carriers or for autologous cells.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Autologous versus Allogeneic Equine Mesenchymal Stem Cells Delivered via Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel for Tendon Healing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the use of allogeneic mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) in veterinary regenerative medicine is attractive due to their off-the-shelf availability, but concerns persist about potential immunogenicity and reduced potency compared to autologous cells. Dr. Badylak's prior work demonstrates the pro-healing immunomodulatory effects of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds and matrix-bound nanovesicles, yet the optimal MSC source for delivery within these ECM-based platforms remains undefined for clinical translation in large animals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that autologous equine MSCs, delivered in an ECM hydrogel, will elicit a superior tendon healing response characterized by enhanced matrix remodeling and reduced inflammation compared to allogeneic MSCs, due to perfect histocompatibility and more robust paracrine signaling.
proposals.method: Using a standardized equine superficial digital flexor tendon injury model, we will compare three treatment groups: 1) ECM hydrogel alone (control), 2) ECM hydrogel + allogeneic MSCs, and 3) ECM hydrogel + autologous MSCs (n=8/group). MSCs will be characterized for immunophenotype and trilineage potential pre-delivery. Healing will be assessed longitudinally via ultrasonography, and tendons harvested at 12 weeks for histomorphometry (collagen organization, vascularity), immunohistochemistry (macrophage polarization: CD206+ vs. iNOS+), and biomechanical testing to failure. A multiplex cytokine assay will profile the local inflammatory milieu at early time points.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide direct, head-to-head preclinical evidence to guide the choice of MSC source for ECM-based therapies in veterinary and potentially human orthopedics. It will elucidate the interplay between cell source and the immunomodulatory ECM scaffold, determining if autologous cells offer a measurable therapeutic advantage that justifies the logistical and cost burdens of their use.
proposals.novelty: While MSC therapy for tendonitis is studied, a direct comparison of cell sources within the specific context of a pro-healing ECM hydrogel delivery system—designed to modulate the recipient immune response—is novel and addresses a critical translational question not resolved in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Long-Term Assessment of Scar Quality and Tissue Biomechanics Following Mesenchymal Stem Cell Delivery on an Extracellular Matrix Scaffold in a Porcine Model of Full-Thickness Wound Healing
proposals.motivation: While mesenchymal stem cell (MSC) therapies show promise for accelerating acute wound closure, their long-term impact on scar quality remains poorly characterized. Pre-2026 clinical translation is hindered by a lack of data on functional outcomes like tissue elasticity, contracture, and cosmetic appearance beyond the initial healing phase. This gap is critical, as the ultimate goal of regenerative medicine is to restore tissue function, not just achieve coverage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the delivery of MSCs on a bioactive extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffold will lead to superior long-term scar quality, characterized by increased tissue elasticity, reduced contracture, and a more organized collagen architecture compared to scaffold alone or standard care, by modulating the late-stage remodeling phase of healing.
proposals.method: A porcine full-thickness excisional wound model will be used. Wounds will be treated with: 1) ECM hydrogel scaffold seeded with allogeneic MSCs, 2) ECM hydrogel alone, or 3) standard dressing (control). After initial closure, animals will be monitored for 6 and 12 months. Outcomes will be assessed via longitudinal ultrasound elastography to measure tissue stiffness, digital image analysis for scar contraction and appearance (POSAS scale), and terminal histological/immunohistochemical analysis (Masson's trichrome, picrosirius red under polarized light) for collagen organization and maturity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, long-term dataset on the biomechanical and architectural quality of scars following MSC-ECM therapy. It will establish critical outcome metrics for evaluating true tissue regeneration versus repair. The findings will directly inform the design of pivotal pre-clinical studies required for regulatory approval of combined cell-matrix therapies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the focus on the long-term remodeling phase using a clinically relevant large animal model and quantitative biomechanical imaging (elastography), moving beyond short-term histology to assess functional tissue restoration, which is largely unexamined in pre-2026 MSC wound healing literature.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Fibrin Gel Formulation to Enhance Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Retention and Pro-Regenerative Activity in a Porcine Wound Model
proposals.motivation: Fibrin gels are widely used as cell delivery vehicles for mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) in regenerative medicine, but poor cell retention and suboptimal matrix signaling often limit therapeutic efficacy. Pre-2026 work, including Dr. Badylak's on extracellular matrix (ECM) hydrogels and matrix-bound nanovesicles, demonstrates that the biophysical and biochemical composition of a scaffold critically directs host immunomodulation and healing. A systematic, data-driven approach to optimize the foundational fibrin carrier is needed to maximize MSC delivery and function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-linear combinations of fibrinogen and thrombin concentrations will create a fibrin gel matrix that optimally balances structural integrity, porosity, and ligand presentation to significantly enhance MSC retention, viability, and paracrine signaling in a complex wound environment.
proposals.method: We will employ a full-factorial Design of Experiments (DoE) approach, varying fibrinogen (20-80 mg/mL) and thrombin (2-20 U/mL) concentrations to fabricate a library of fibrin gels. Each formulation will be characterized for polymerization kinetics, compressive modulus, and fiber architecture (SEM). Human MSCs will be encapsulated, and in vitro assays will measure viability (Live/Dead), retention (quantitative DNA assay under flow), and secretome (multiplex ELISA for VEGF, HGF, IL-10). The top three formulations will be tested in a porcine full-thickness wound model, with outcomes assessed via bioluminescent imaging (for cell retention), histology, and quantification of macrophage polarization (iNOS/CD206).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a definitive, empirically optimized fibrin formulation protocol for MSC delivery, moving beyond anecdotal recipes. It will directly link specific gel parameters to in vivo cell retention and pro-healing immunomodulation, providing a tunable platform technology that can be augmented with future ECM-derived components, such as matrix-bound nanovesicles.
proposals.novelty: While fibrin is commonly used, a rigorous DoE approach to deconvolute the synergistic effects of its core components on MSC fate in vivo is lacking. This proposal uniquely integrates quantitative in vitro screening with validation in a clinically relevant large animal model, focusing on the critical outcome of cell retention driven by matrix mechanics.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Mapping of the Immunomodulatory and Pro-Regenerative Effects of Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel on Diabetic Wound Healing
proposals.motivation: Chronic wounds, particularly diabetic ulcers, represent a major clinical burden due to a dysregulated, pro-inflammatory microenvironment that stalls healing. While extracellular matrix (ECM)-based scaffolds, such as Dr. Badylak's urinary bladder matrix (UBM) hydrogel, have shown clinical promise in promoting constructive remodeling, their precise mechanism of action on specific wound cell populations remains incompletely defined. A high-resolution understanding of how ECM scaffolds modulate the heterogeneous cellular landscape of a healing wound is critical for optimizing their therapeutic application and developing next-generation biomaterials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treatment with ECM hydrogel will induce a distinct, pro-regenerative transcriptional program in wound-resident cells—including macrophages, fibroblasts, and endothelial cells—shifting the immune milieu from a chronic inflammatory state towards a restorative, M2-like macrophage-dominated and tissue-forming phenotype, as revealed by single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq).
proposals.method: We will establish full-thickness excisional wounds in a diabetic (db/db) mouse model, treating wounds with UBM hydrogel or saline control. At key time points (days 3, 7, 14), wound beds will be harvested, dissociated into single-cell suspensions, and processed using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform for scRNA-seq. Cell Ranger will be used for initial data processing, alignment, and UMI counting. Subsequent bioinformatic analysis will involve Seurat for clustering, cell type annotation via canonical markers, and differential gene expression analysis to identify hydrogel-induced transcriptional changes within each cluster. Trajectory analysis using Monocle 3 will be applied to infer cellular state transitions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, single-cell atlas of cellular responses to an ECM hydrogel in diabetic wound healing. It will definitively identify which cell types are the primary targets of ECM-mediated immunomodulation and delineate the specific pro-healing gene expression signatures they adopt. The findings will directly inform the rational design of future matrix-based therapies and combination treatments aimed at correcting pathological cellular states in chronic wounds.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Badylak's prior work has demonstrated the overall immunomodulatory capacity of ECM degradation products and matrix-bound nanovesicles, applying scRNA-seq to the healing wound niche represents a novel, systems-level approach to deconvolve these effects with unprecedented cellular specificity, moving beyond bulk tissue analysis or predefined cellular assays.

proposals.title: A Microneedle-Array-Delivered Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel for the Spatially Patterned Delivery of Mesenchymal Stromal Cells to Promote Wound Healing
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cells (MSCs) are potent modulators of the wound microenvironment, promoting angiogenesis and immunomodulation, but their clinical delivery remains a challenge. Current methods like direct injection are invasive, cause cell loss, and lack spatial control over cell placement. A minimally invasive, patterned delivery system could enhance MSC retention and therapeutic efficacy in complex wounds, aligning with the clinical translation goals of regenerative medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dissolvable microneedle array fabricated from porcine urinary bladder extracellular matrix (UBM-ECM) hydrogel will enable the minimally invasive, spatially patterned delivery of viable MSCs into dermal wounds, resulting in superior cell retention, modulated host immune response, and accelerated healing compared to standard injection.
proposals.method: We will fabricate microneedle arrays using a micromolding technique with a UBM-ECM hydrogel, characterized pre-2026 for its pro-healing properties. MSCs will be incorporated into the hydrogel matrix prior to molding. In vitro, array dissolution kinetics and MSC viability post-fabrication and release will be assessed. In a rodent full-thickness excisional wound model, arrays will be applied and compared to PBS control, ECM hydrogel alone, and MSC suspension injection. Healing will be tracked macroscopically and histologically, with immunofluorescence used to quantify MSC retention and characterize macrophage polarization (M1/M2) at the wound site.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, integrated platform combining Badylak's ECM scaffold technology with advanced transdermal delivery for cell therapy. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on the feasibility and efficacy of patterned MSC delivery, potentially leading to a new class of 'smart' bandages for complex wound management. The findings will contribute to the fields of biomaterials, drug/cell delivery, and regenerative medicine.
proposals.novelty: While microneedles have been used for drug and vaccine delivery, their application for the patterned delivery of therapeutic cells within a bioactive ECM hydrogel scaffold is novel. This approach uniquely merges the immunomodulatory and structural cues of the ECM with precise spatial placement of living, therapeutic cells.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Profiling of the MSC Paracrine Response to ECM-Derived Scaffolds Using High-Plex Cytokine Capture Arrays
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) secretome is a primary mediator of their immunomodulatory and pro-regenerative effects in wound healing. However, the dynamic, multi-factorial cytokine response of MSCs to specific extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds—a core technology in Badylak's lab—remains poorly characterized. A comprehensive, quantitative secretome profile is needed to rationally design and optimize next-generation ECM-based therapies by linking scaffold composition to specific immunomodulatory outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct ECM scaffold compositions (e.g., urinary bladder matrix vs. small intestinal submucosa) will elicit unique, time-dependent paracrine signatures from MSCs, and that these signatures correlate with specific macrophage polarization states and functional wound healing outcomes in vivo.
proposals.method: Human bone marrow-derived MSCs will be cultured on characterized ECM hydrogel scaffolds derived from porcine urinary bladder and small intestine. Conditioned media will be collected at 24, 48, and 72 hours. Secreted factors will be profiled using Luminex xMAP high-plex bead-based immunoassays, quantifying a panel of 40+ analytes (e.g., IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α, VEGF, HGF, PGE2). Data will be analyzed via principal component analysis and hierarchical clustering to identify scaffold-specific secretory signatures. Key signatures will be functionally validated using in vitro macrophage polarization assays and a rodent dermal wound model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-resolution map linking ECM scaffold bioactivity to MSC paracrine output, moving beyond single-factor analyses. It will provide a data-driven framework for selecting or engineering ECM scaffolds to elicit desired therapeutic secretomes. The findings will directly inform the design of combination 'scaffold-plus-cell' therapies with enhanced and predictable immunomodulatory potency.
proposals.novelty: While cytokine arrays are established in immunology, their application to systematically deconvolute the signaling dialogue between ECM scaffolds and MSCs is novel. This approach treats the scaffold not just as a passive carrier but as an active instructor of cellular secretome, a concept critical for advanced therapeutic design.

proposals.title: Preconditioning of Mesenchymal Stromal Cells with a Chronic Wound Matrix Hydrogel Enhances Pro-Healing Immunomodulation in a Diabetic Mouse Model
proposals.motivation: Mesenchymal stromal cell (MSC) therapies for chronic wounds show inconsistent efficacy, partly due to the hostile, pro-inflammatory wound microenvironment that can suppress MSC function. Pre-2026 work by Badylak's group and others has demonstrated that the extracellular matrix (ECM) from healing tissues contains bioactive cues that direct constructive remodeling, while non-healing wounds exhibit a distinct, dysregulated molecular profile. Leveraging these pathological signals to prime MSCs ex vivo could better prepare them for the challenges of the chronic wound bed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that preconditioning MSCs with a hydrogel derived from the decellularized ECM of a non-healing wound model will enhance their therapeutic potency by upregulating pro-regenerative paracrine signaling and immunomodulatory capacity, leading to improved wound healing outcomes compared to naive MSCs.
proposals.method: We will create a chronic wound model in diabetic db/db mice and harvest non-healing wound tissue. This tissue will be decellularized and processed into a solubilized ECM hydrogel following established protocols. Human bone marrow-derived MSCs will be cultured in 3D within this chronic wound matrix hydrogel for 48-72 hours ex vivo. Preconditioned MSCs will be delivered to fresh wounds in a new cohort of diabetic mice. Outcomes will be assessed via wound closure kinetics, histology, and multiplex analysis of wound fluid cytokines, comparing to groups treated with naive MSCs, hydrogel alone, or the preconditioning matrix derived from healthy skin.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, clinically translatable method to enhance MSC therapy by using disease-specific ECM as a preconditioning stimulus. It will provide mechanistic insight into how pathological microenvironmental cues can be repurposed to train therapeutic cells. The findings could lead to optimized, 'next-generation' cell therapies with improved consistency for complex wound healing.
proposals.novelty: While ECM hydrogels are used as delivery scaffolds, using a pathological ECM specifically as an ex vivo preconditioning tool to educate MSCs is a novel paradigm. This approach directly leverages Badylak's expertise in matrix biology to solve a key limitation in cell therapy, moving beyond the scaffold's structural role to a bioactive instructional one.

proposals.title: Comparative Evaluation of ECM Scaffold Biophysical and Immunomodulatory Properties for Targeted Clinical Application in Hernia Repair and Musculotendinous Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Decellularized extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds are widely used in regenerative medicine, but clinical outcomes can be inconsistent. This variability may stem from applying a generic scaffold to diverse anatomical sites with distinct mechanical and biological requirements. A systematic, comparative assessment of scaffold performance in models of structurally demanding soft tissue repairs, such as hernia (tension-bearing) and musculotendinous reconstruction (load-transferring), is needed to inform scaffold selection and design for specific applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the in vivo remodeling outcome of an ECM scaffold is dictated by a combination of its source-tissue-specific biophysical properties and its ability to orchestrate a pro-regenerative macrophage response, and that optimal performance for hernia repair will require different scaffold characteristics than for musculotendinous reconstruction.
proposals.method: We will fabricate and characterize ECM scaffolds from porcine dermis, urinary bladder matrix (UBM), and small intestinal submucosa (SIS) for thickness, porosity, suture retention strength, and tensile modulus. Using a rat model, scaffolds will be implanted in two defect models: a full-thickness abdominal wall defect (hernia) and a surgically created Achilles tendon gap. Outcomes at 14 and 56 days will include histomorphometry (cellular infiltration, neotissue organization, vascularity), immunohistochemistry for macrophage phenotypes (iNOS+/CD206+), and biomechanical testing of the explanted construct. A multivariate analysis will correlate initial scaffold properties with in vivo remodeling metrics for each application.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a preclinical framework for matching specific ECM scaffold properties to clinical indications based on functional requirements. It will provide data-driven guidelines for surgeons and manufacturers on scaffold selection for mechanically distinct repairs. The findings will advance the field from a one-scaffold-fits-all paradigm towards a precision medicine approach in scaffold-based reconstruction.
proposals.novelty: While individual scaffolds have been tested in singular models, a direct, head-to-head comparative study linking a suite of quantifiable scaffold properties to distinct functional outcomes in two demanding models is lacking. This approach moves beyond reporting scaffold efficacy to identifying the critical material determinants of success for specific anatomical applications.

proposals.title: The Influence of Porcine Breed-Specific Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds on Host Cell Recruitment, Vascularization, and Remodeling in a Rodent Model of Volumetric Muscle Loss
proposals.motivation: Decellularized extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds are widely used in regenerative medicine, but their biological performance is known to vary with tissue source and processing. Pre-2026 studies by Badylak and others have shown that ECM composition and bound nanovesicles critically influence macrophage polarization and downstream healing outcomes. However, the specific impact of donor animal breed—a variable affecting tissue architecture, growth factor content, and matrix stiffness—on the fundamental processes of cell infiltration and scaffold remodeling remains poorly characterized, limiting scaffold optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ECM scaffolds derived from different porcine breeds will exhibit significant differences in their ability to recruit host progenitor cells, promote neovascularization, and undergo constructive remodeling in vivo, due to breed-specific variations in matrix-bound vesicle content, structural proteins, and bioactive molecule retention.
proposals.method: We will fabricate urinary bladder matrix (UBM) hydrogel scaffolds from three genetically distinct porcine breeds (e.g., Yorkshire, Duroc, Pietrain). These scaffolds will be implanted in a rat volumetric muscle loss model. Host cell recruitment will be quantified via longitudinal in vivo imaging (e.g., bioluminescence for tagged progenitor cells) and endpoint histomorphometry for pan-leukocyte (CD45+), macrophage (CD68+, iNOS+, CD206+), and endothelial (CD31+) cell infiltration. Scaffold degradation and neotissue formation will be assessed using picrosirius red polarization for collagen alignment, immunohistochemistry for myosin heavy chain, and hydroxyproline assay for collagen content at 1, 2, and 4-week timepoints. Matrix-bound nanovesicle (MBV) profiles from each breed will be characterized via nanoparticle tracking analysis and proteomics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between donor animal breed and the in vivo host response to ECM scaffolds, providing a data-driven rationale for source tissue selection. It will identify specific matrix characteristics (e.g., MBV cargo, collagen type ratios) that correlate with superior cell recruitment and vascularization, offering new criteria for scaffold quality control and design.
proposals.novelty: While scaffold source species (porcine, bovine) has been studied, systematically evaluating intra-species genetic breed as a critical variable affecting host-scaffold interaction is a novel and translationally relevant approach to standardizing and improving ECM-based therapeutics.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening of Cell Phenotype on Micro-Patterned Placental Extracellular Matrix Patches
proposals.motivation: The extracellular matrix (ECM) is a critical regulator of cell behavior, but its effects are highly source- and composition-dependent. Pre-2026 work from the Badylak lab and others demonstrates that ECM from specific tissues (e.g., urinary bladder, small intestine) elicits distinct, therapeutically valuable cellular responses, such as pro-healing immunomodulation. However, screening these complex, tissue-specific ECM materials for specific applications (e.g., cancer inhibition, epithelialization) is limited by low-throughput, macroscale formats that consume significant amounts of precious decellularized ECM.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that micro-patterned patches of decellularized placental ECM, fabricated using high-resolution techniques, will retain bioactivity and enable the high-throughput identification of specific ECM compositions and topographies that direct desired cell phenotypes, such as anti-proliferative effects on cancer cells or pro-migratory effects on epithelial cells.
proposals.method: Human placental tissue will be decellularized and processed into a solubilized ECM preparation. Using soft lithography and micro-contact printing, this ECM will be deposited in an array of uniform, sub-millimeter patches (e.g., 200µm diameter circles) onto cell-repellent hydrogel substrates within standard multi-well plates. Automated liquid handling will seed defined cell types (e.g., esophageal cancer cells, primary fibroblasts) onto these arrays. High-content imaging platforms will be employed to quantify cell responses—including proliferation, migration, morphology, and marker expression—in a spatially resolved manner across hundreds of replicate patches per experimental condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for the rapid, multiplexed screening of tissue-specific ECM bioactivity, directly linking microscale ECM composition to quantifiable cellular outcomes. It will generate a foundational dataset mapping placental ECM properties to functional cell responses, accelerating the rational design of ECM-based scaffolds for regenerative medicine and oncology. The platform itself will be a new tool for the field to deconvolute the effects of ECM biochemistry and topography.
proposals.novelty: While ECM hydrogels and scaffolds are well-studied, applying micro-patterning and high-throughput screening methodologies to create spatially defined, miniaturized patches of intact, tissue-specific ECM for functional cellular screening is a novel integration of biofabrication and systems biology approaches within the ECM field.

proposals.title: A Bioactive, Matrix-Bound Nanovesicle-Enriched Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel Scaffold for Host-Driven Cardiac Muscle Regeneration Without Exogenous Cell Delivery
proposals.motivation: Current cell-seeded scaffolds for cardiac repair face significant translational hurdles, including cell sourcing, viability, and immunogenicity. A truly cell-free, off-the-shelf scaffold that actively recruits and instructs host cells would represent a paradigm shift. Pre-2026 work from the Badylak lab demonstrates the potent immunomodulatory and pro-healing effects of extracellular matrix (ECM) degradation products and matrix-bound nanovesicles (MBVs), providing a foundation for designing such bioactive materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decellularized myocardial ECM hydrogel, engineered to retain and present a defined profile of MBVs and cryptic peptides from controlled enzymatic degradation, will create a microenvironment that selectively recruits endogenous progenitor cells and macrophages to promote functional cardiac muscle regeneration in a rodent myocardial infarction model.
proposals.method: We will fabricate scaffolds from porcine myocardial ECM subjected to tailored, partial enzymatic digestion to liberate specific cryptic peptides known to influence cell migration and proliferation. MBVs will be isolated from the same ECM source via established centrifugation protocols and physically incorporated into the hydrogel during gelation. Scaffold bioactivity will be quantified in vitro using transwell assays for cardiac progenitor cell and macrophage recruitment. The optimized scaffold will be evaluated in a rat model of induced myocardial infarction, with outcomes assessed at 4 and 8 weeks via echocardiography, histology for cardiomyocyte infiltration and angiogenesis, and flow cytometry for immune cell polarization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive proof-of-concept for a fully acellular, host-recruiting scaffold for cardiac repair, moving the field beyond cell-seeding strategies. It will establish a direct link between scaffold processing parameters (enzymatic digestion, MBV incorporation) and in vivo regenerative outcomes. The findings could yield a new class of clinically viable, off-the-shelf biomaterials for treating ischemic heart disease.
proposals.novelty: While ECM hydrogels are established, the novelty lies in the intentional co-presentation of cryptic ECM peptides and MBVs as a unified instructional cue, designed not just as a passive scaffold but as an active recruiter and director of the host's innate repair mechanisms, challenging the necessity of complex exogenous cell therapy.

proposals.title: Evaluation of Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds Seeded with Meniscal Fibrochondrocytes versus Articular Chondrocytes for Cartilage Repair
proposals.motivation: Autologous chondrocyte implantation (ACI) remains the clinical gold standard for cell-based cartilage repair, but it has significant limitations, including donor site morbidity, dedifferentiation during expansion, and the production of fibrocartilage. Pre-2026 research by Badylak and others has demonstrated the potent immunomodulatory and pro-regenerative effects of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds, suggesting the scaffold itself may orchestrate repair. This creates an opportunity to challenge the necessity of using expanded articular chondrocytes as the default cell source.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a decellularized ECM scaffold seeded with meniscal fibrochondrocytes (MFCs) will promote superior hyaline-like cartilage repair in a critical-size osteochondral defect model compared to the same scaffold seeded with expanded articular chondrocytes (ACs), due to the MFCs' inherent matrix synthesis profile and synergistic interaction with the ECM-derived signals.
proposals.method: We will fabricate porous, urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogel scaffolds as per established Badylak lab protocols. Autologous articular chondrocytes and meniscal fibrochondrocytes will be isolated and expanded from a porcine model. Cells will be seeded onto scaffolds and characterized in vitro for viability, gene expression (COL2A1, ACAN, SOX9), and matrix deposition. For in vivo testing, critical-size osteochondral defects in the porcine femoral condyle will be treated with: 1) UBM scaffold + MFCs, 2) UBM scaffold + ACs, 3) UBM scaffold alone, and 4) microfracture. Outcomes at 3 and 6 months will be assessed via histology (Safranin-O, collagen II/I immunohistochemistry), biomechanical testing, and quantitative MRI (T2 mapping).
proposals.contribution: This work will directly test the paradigm that autologous articular chondrocytes are the optimal cell for cartilage repair, providing comparative pre-clinical data for an alternative, more readily available cell source. It will advance the field by defining the combinatorial effect of a specific ECM scaffold with a specific progenitor cell type, moving toward a more holistic, scaffold-driven therapeutic strategy.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds have been tested with chondrocytes, a direct comparative study challenging the supremacy of articular chondrocytes with meniscal fibrochondrocytes on a pro-healing UBM scaffold is novel. The study uniquely leverages the immunomodulatory properties of the ECM to potentially enhance the performance of a non-traditional cell source.

proposals.title: Direct Implantation of Adipose-Derived Stromal Cell-Seeded Extracellular Matrix Hydrogels for Volumetric Muscle Loss: A Comparative Study of In Vitro Pre-Culture vs. Immediate In Vivo Engraftment
proposals.motivation: Current regenerative strategies for volumetric muscle loss (VML) often involve seeding scaffolds with cells and performing a prolonged in vitro culture to promote tissue formation prior to implantation.
