This step is costly, time-consuming, and risks contamination or phenotypic drift. A streamlined protocol enabling direct implantation of cell-laden scaffolds would accelerate clinical translation and reduce manufacturing complexity, aligning with the goal of developing practical, off-the-shelf-like regenerative therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a urinary bladder matrix (UBM) hydrogel, laden with freshly isolated adipose-derived stromal cells (ASCs) and implanted directly into a VML defect, will promote equivalent or superior functional muscle recovery and pro-regenerative macrophage polarization compared to a pre-cultured cell-scaffold construct, due to the immediate in vivo presentation of matrix-bound nanovesicles and bioactive cues.
proposals.method: Using a rat VML model, we will compare three groups: 1) UBM hydrogel alone, 2) UBM hydrogel pre-seeded with ASCs and cultured in vitro for 7 days, and 3) UBM hydrogel immediately implanted after ASC seeding (time-zero implantation). ASCs will be isolated from syngeneic rats. Outcomes at 4 and 8 weeks will include functional analysis (force generation), histology (immunofluorescence for myosin heavy chain, Pax7, CD206, CD86), and quantification of regenerated muscle area. Luminex assays will profile local cytokine levels at early time points.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critical pre-clinical evidence for simplifying tissue engineering protocols, potentially eliminating a major logistical and financial barrier. It will advance our understanding of how the in vivo microenvironment, particularly the immunomodulatory signals from ECM scaffolds, directs freshly delivered progenitor cells, contributing to the paradigm of in situ tissue engineering.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Badylak's work has shown the standalone efficacy of ECM scaffolds and the utility of matrix-bound nanovesicles, directly testing the necessity of the standard pre-culture phase for cell delivery is a significant and pragmatic departure from conventional tissue engineering practice.

proposals.title: Long-Term Evaluation of Extracellular Matrix-Based Meniscal Implants: Durability, Joint Preservation, and Host Immunomodulatory Response in a Large Animal Model
proposals.motivation: Meniscal injuries are common and lead to joint degeneration. While short-term studies (e.g., 4 months) show promise for ECM-derived scaffolds in meniscal repair, the long-term durability of these implants and their ability to prevent osteoarthritis remain unknown. A comprehensive, long-term evaluation is critical to translate these promising scaffolds into reliable clinical solutions for joint preservation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a properly remodeled, site-appropriate extracellular matrix meniscal implant will demonstrate sustained mechanical integrity, support native tissue ingrowth, and promote a pro-regenerative immunomodulatory environment over 12-18 months, thereby preserving joint cartilage health.
proposals.method: We will use a well-established ovine model of meniscal resection. The defect will be treated with a commercially available, decellularized ECM scaffold (e.g., Menaflex or a similar device). Animals will be evaluated at 6, 12, and 18 months post-implantation. Outcomes will include longitudinal MRI for implant morphology and cartilage health, gait analysis, terminal mechanical testing of the implant and adjacent cartilage, and comprehensive histomorphometry with specific immunohistochemistry for macrophage polarization (M1/M2 markers) and matrix-bound nanovesicle (MBV) presence to assess the sustained immunomodulatory microenvironment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first long-term, multi-modal dataset on the performance of an ECM meniscal scaffold, defining its failure modes and success criteria beyond early integration. It will establish a direct link between long-term implant remodeling, the local immune environment, and cartilage protection, offering critical pre-clinical evidence for regulatory approval and clinical adoption.
proposals.novelty: While short-term integration is documented, this work uniquely focuses on the long-term *remodeling fate* of the ECM implant and its sustained capacity for immunomodulation via MBVs, correlating these biological processes directly with objective measures of joint preservation over an extended timeframe.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Surgical Fixation Techniques for Extracellular Matrix-Based, Cell-Seeded Implants in a Rodent Articular Cartilage Defect Model
proposals.motivation: The clinical translation of cell-seeded extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds for cartilage repair is hindered by inconsistent surgical outcomes. Implant delamination, poor integration, and mechanical instability post-implantation compromise chondroprotection and long-term function. A systematic, comparative study of fixation methods is urgently needed to establish a reliable surgical protocol that maximizes implant integrity and biological performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sutureless, bioadhesive fixation technique will provide superior implant stability, integration, and chondrogenic outcomes compared to traditional suture or fibrin glue methods in a load-bearing cartilage defect model, by minimizing mechanical disruption to the seeded cells and the ECM scaffold's native bioactivity.
proposals.method: We will fabricate cell-seeded implants using porcine urinary bladder ECM hydrogel seeded with primary chondrocytes. In a rat femoral condyle critical-size defect model, we will rigorously compare three fixation techniques: 1) traditional interrupted sutures, 2) commercial fibrin sealant, and 3) a novel, mechanically tuned ECM-based bioadhesive. Primary outcomes, assessed at 4, 8, and 12 weeks, will include implant retention (micro-CT), integration strength (push-out test), and chondroprotection (histology for collagen type II, GAG content, and subchondral bone architecture). Secondary outcomes will assess local immunomodulation via histomorphometry for macrophage polarization (M1/M2).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative comparison of surgical fixation strategies for ECM-based, cell-seeded cartilage implants, identifying a technique that optimizes mechanical stability and biological integration. The findings will deliver a validated surgical protocol to enhance preclinical-to-clinical translation and establish design principles for next-generation bioadhesives tailored to ECM scaffold properties.
proposals.novelty: While Badylak's group has pioneered ECM scaffold applications and demonstrated their immunomodulatory capacity (e.g., matrix-bound nanovesicles), the critical variable of surgical fixation technique has not been systematically studied in the context of cell-seeded constructs. This proposal uniquely bridges scaffold science with practical surgical optimization to solve a key translational bottleneck.

proposals.title: Optimizing Autologous Cell Seeding of Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds for Enhanced Cartilage Regeneration
proposals.motivation: Despite the clinical success of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds in soft tissue repair, their application for functional cartilage regeneration remains suboptimal. A key unresolved challenge is the identification of the most effective autologous cell source and seeding parameters to promote robust, hyaline-like cartilaginous tissue formation within these scaffolds. This study directly addresses this gap, which is critical for advancing treatments for articular cartilage defects and tracheal reconstruction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that seeding ECM scaffolds with specific autologous cell types (e.g., chondrocytes vs. bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stromal cells) at a defined, optimized density will synergistically leverage the scaffold's pro-regenerative signaling to enhance chondrogenic differentiation and extracellular matrix deposition, leading to superior structural and mechanical properties of the engineered tissue.
proposals.method: We will fabricate porous, urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM scaffolds. Autologous chondrocytes and bone marrow-derived MSCs will be isolated from a porcine model and seeded onto scaffolds at three densities (low, medium, high). Constructs will be cultured in a chondrogenic medium in a bioreactor. Outcomes will be assessed at 2, 4, and 6 weeks via histology (Safranin-O, collagen II/I), biochemical assays (GAG/DNA content), mechanical testing, and gene expression analysis of chondrogenic markers (SOX9, ACAN, COL2A1).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational protocol for cell-seeded ECM scaffold-based cartilage engineering, directly linking specific cell sourcing and seeding parameters to functional tissue outcomes. It will provide critical pre-clinical data to inform the design of more effective, patient-specific regenerative therapies for cartilage repair, moving beyond acellular scaffold approaches.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are often studied acellularly or with single cell types, this study systematically compares the regenerative potential of primary chondrocytes versus MSCs within the same ECM scaffold, explicitly testing the interaction between cell source and seeding density—a non-obvious optimization crucial for clinical translation.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Control of Myogenesis via a Porcine Urinary Bladder Matrix Scaffold with Sequentially Released Growth Factors
proposals.motivation: Current regenerative medicine strategies using extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds, such as Dr. Badylak's porcine urinary bladder matrix (UBM), provide a pro-healing microenvironment but offer limited control over the complex, sequential signaling required for functional tissue regeneration. Guiding stem cell differentiation into organized, multi-nucleated myofibers for volumetric muscle loss (VML) repair remains a significant clinical challenge. Integrating controlled drug delivery principles into ECM scaffolds presents a promising solution to orchestrate the myogenic cascade.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a UBM hydrogel scaffold engineered to sequentially release IGF-1 (proliferation/migration) followed by a delayed release of BMP-2 (differentiation) will significantly enhance the formation of aligned, contractile myotubes from seeded myoblasts in vitro and improve functional muscle regeneration in a rodent VML model compared to UBM alone.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a dual-compartment UBM hydrogel system. Fast-release IGF-1 will be encapsulated in surface-adsorbed gelatin microparticles, while delayed-release BMP-2 will be loaded into poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles embedded within the hydrogel bulk. Release kinetics will be characterized via ELISA. In vitro, C2C12 myoblasts will be cultured on scaffolds, with myotube alignment, fusion index, and contractile protein expression (MyoD, myogenin, MHC) analyzed. In vivo, scaffolds will be implanted in a rat quadriceps VML defect, with functional recovery assessed by force transduction and histomorphometry at 4 and 12 weeks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, bioinspired ECM scaffold platform capable of delivering precise temporal cues to guide tissue formation. It will provide critical proof-of-concept that sequential growth factor release from a clinical-grade ECM material can significantly enhance the structural and functional outcomes of muscle regeneration, moving beyond passive scaffolding to active biological instruction.
proposals.novelty: While growth factor delivery from synthetic polymers is established, the integration of sophisticated, sequential release kinetics into a naturally derived, immunomodulatory ECM scaffold (like UBM) to recapitulate the dynamic signaling of development is novel. This approach combines the 'instructive' power of drug delivery with the 'permissive' pro-regenerative niche of ECM.

proposals.title: Scalable Manufacturing and Terminal Sterilization of Decellularized Extracellular Matrix Meniscal Scaffolds for Human Clinical Translation
proposals.motivation: Meniscal injuries are common and lead to osteoarthritis, but current treatments, including partial meniscectomy and allografts, have significant limitations. Decellularized extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds have shown promise in preclinical studies for meniscus regeneration, but translation is hindered by the lack of reproducible, scalable fabrication and validated terminal sterilization processes for human-sized, anatomically complex implants. Establishing these processes is a critical, unresolved barrier to clinical application.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of large-animal (e.g., porcine) source tissue processing, controlled decellularization and crosslinking, and a terminal sterilization method (e.g., electron beam irradiation) can be developed to produce sterile, human-scale meniscal ECM scaffolds that retain critical bioinductive properties and meet regulatory standards for implantation.
proposals.method: We will adapt Badylak lab's established protocols for ECM scaffold fabrication to a scalable bioreactor system for processing multiple, large porcine menisci simultaneously. Decellularization efficacy, mechanical properties (compressive and tensile modulus), and retention of key matrix-bound nanovesicles (MBVs) and collagen architecture will be quantified. Terminal sterilization via electron beam irradiation at varying doses (15-25 kGy) will be systematically evaluated against ethylene oxide for its impact on scaffold sterility, mechanical integrity, and in vitro bioactivity (e.g., macrophage polarization towards an M2 pro-healing phenotype).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable manufacturing pipeline for a clinically relevant meniscal ECM implant, directly addressing a key translational gap. It will yield critical data on the effects of terminal sterilization on ECM scaffold bioactivity, informing regulatory submissions and Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) production. The resulting protocol will be a template for scaling other complex ECM-based implants.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are widely studied, a systematic, integrated approach to scaling their fabrication *and* validating a terminal sterilization method that preserves their immunomodulatory (e.g., MBV-driven) bioactivity for a load-bearing structure like the meniscus is novel and essential for clinical adoption.

proposals.title: A Porcine Urinary Bladder Matrix-Derived Hydrogel Barrier Enhances Intrinsic Healing and Prevents Degeneration Following Meniscal Repair in a Preclinical Model
proposals.motivation: Meniscal tears are common and often lead to progressive cartilage degeneration and osteoarthritis. Current surgical repair techniques have high failure rates, particularly in the avascular zone, due to poor intrinsic healing and persistent tissue loss at the repair site. A strategy to actively prevent this post-repair tissue loss, rather than merely replacing the meniscus after failure, represents a critical unmet clinical need in orthopedic surgery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a bioactive extracellular matrix (ECM) hydrogel as a regenerative barrier over a surgically repaired meniscal tear will modulate the local inflammatory and fibrogenic response, enhance endogenous repair mechanisms, and prevent tissue resorption and degeneration at the repair site.
proposals.method: We will utilize a well-established rodent model of meniscal repair in the avascular zone. Following surgical repair of a standardized tear, the experimental group will receive an application of a pre-clinically validated, matrix-bound nanovesicle (MBV)-containing urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogel as a conformal barrier over the repair site. Control groups will receive repair alone or repair with a non-bioactive hydrogel. Healing will be assessed at 4, 8, and 12 weeks via histology (Safranin-O, Picrosirius Red), immunohistochemistry (for macrophages, collagen types I/II), biomechanical push-out testing, and micro-CT to quantify tissue loss and integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for a 'regenerative barrier' approach in meniscal healing, directly translating Badylak lab expertise in ECM-mediated immunomodulation to a new orthopedic application. It will provide mechanistic data on how ECM hydrogels influence the meniscal repair microenvironment and generate preclinical evidence to support a novel, off-the-shelf adjuvant therapy to improve the success of primary meniscal repair surgeries.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are used for meniscus replacement, their application as a protective, pro-healing barrier immediately following primary surgical repair to prevent tissue loss is novel. This approach leverages the dual bioactivity of the UBM hydrogel (structural support and MBV-driven immunomodulation) for a preventative, rather than reconstructive, regenerative medicine strategy.

proposals.title: A Degradable Extracellular Matrix Scaffold Engineered for Spatiotemporal Control of Host Cell Recruitment and Tissue Regeneration
proposals.motivation: Current regenerative medicine scaffolds often persist as long-term foreign bodies, potentially causing chronic inflammation or mechanical mismatch. The ideal scaffold should orchestrate the body's innate healing response and then vanish, leaving only functional native tissue. This 'inverse problem'—designing a material for its own disappearance via active recruitment and instruction of host cells—addresses a critical gap in achieving scarless, functional tissue restoration.
proposals.hypothesis: An extracellular matrix (ECM)-based scaffold, with precisely tuned degradation kinetics and the controlled release of matrix-bound nanovesicles (MBVs), will sequentially recruit specific host progenitor and immune cell populations to a defect site, guide their organization into functional tissue, and fully degrade, resulting in regeneration without a permanent synthetic footprint.
proposals.method: We will fabricate porous urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogels with crosslinking densities modulated by genipin concentration to achieve degradation times of 7, 14, and 28 days in vitro. MBVs will be isolated from the same UBM source and encapsulated within the hydrogel. In a rodent full-thickness abdominal wall defect model, we will implant these scaffolds and use longitudinal in vivo imaging, flow cytometry of digested explants, and histomorphometry to correlate scaffold degradation timeline with the sequential recruitment of M2 macrophages, mesenchymal stromal cells, and endothelial cells, and the subsequent deposition and organization of new, vascularized tissue.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal relationship between scaffold degradation rate and the quality of de novo tissue formation. It will provide a design framework for 'vanishing' scaffolds that function as transient instructional platforms. The findings could shift the paradigm from implanting a permanent construct to implanting a transient, cell-instructive signal.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are known to degrade and recruit cells, this proposal intentionally engineers rapid, predictable degradation as the primary driver of regeneration, not a passive byproduct. It uniquely combines tunable ECM hydrogel mechanics with the sustained, localized release of endogenous MBVs to guide the entire healing cascade.

proposals.title: A Standardized Surgical Protocol for Implanting Biofabricated Extracellular Matrix Membranes to Optimize Integrity and Function in Complex Co-culture Tissue Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has demonstrated the immense potential of decellularized extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds and hydrogels in regenerative medicine and in vitro tissue modeling. However, the translation of biofabricated ECM membranes into complex, multi-layered co-culture systems (e.g., for barrier interfaces) is hindered by a lack of standardized surgical handling techniques. Variability in placement, folding, and fixation methods during implantation directly impacts membrane integrity, cellular seeding, and ultimate functional mimicry, limiting reproducibility and clinical predictability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, optimized surgical protocol for the implantation of biofabricated ECM membranes will maximize structural integrity and promote superior cellular interaction and barrier function across diverse complex co-culture tissue models, compared to ad-hoc or model-specific techniques.
proposals.method: We will utilize commercially sourced, lyophilized porcine urinary bladder ECM (UBM) processed into uniform hydrogel membranes. Using a modular co-culture chamber, we will systematically test surgical variables: membrane hydration state (pre-hydrated vs. dry placement), fixation method (fibrin sealant, mechanical clamping, partial embedding), and folding technique for creating multi-layered interfaces. These parameters will be applied to three established co-culture models: a blood-brain barrier (endothelial/astrocyte), an alveolar-capillary unit (epithelial/endothelial), and an intestinal-vascular interface. Primary outcome measures will include membrane integrity (histology, confocal microscopy), cellular viability and infiltration (Live/Dead assay, immunohistochemistry), and functional metrics (transepithelial/transendothelial electrical resistance, permeability assays).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorously defined, cross-platform surgical protocol for ECM membrane implantation, directly enhancing the reproducibility and physiological relevance of advanced in vitro co-culture systems. It will establish critical best practices for scaffold handling that bridge in vitro modeling and future in vivo surgical application, accelerating the development of ECM-based regenerative therapies for complex tissue interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While prior Badylak lab work has focused on ECM composition and immunomodulation, this proposal uniquely applies a surgical engineering lens to in vitro scaffold deployment, seeking a universal handling standard rather than a material-specific solution. The systematic translation of macro-surgical principles to micro-scale tissue modeling is a novel and necessary step for the field.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Microfluidic Platform to Assess the Mechanobiological Determinants of Scaffold-Free Adipose Graft Survival and Vascularization
proposals.motivation: Autologous fat grafting is a common reconstructive procedure, but unpredictable graft resorption due to poor vascularization and mechanical stress remains a major clinical limitation. While extracellular matrix (ECM)-based scaffolds can improve outcomes, the fundamental question of whether a permanent scaffold is necessary for graft survival under physiological conditions is unresolved. Current static in vitro models and animal studies cannot adequately replicate the dynamic mechanical and biochemical microenvironment of a graft site, hindering mechanistic insight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaffold-free, pre-vascularized adipose tissue constructs will achieve volumetric retention and functional vascular integration within a dynamic, physiologically relevant microfluidic environment that provides cyclic strain and perfusion, and that this outcome will be mediated by mechanosensitive, pro-healing immunomodulation akin to that induced by matrix-bound nanovesicles.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a multi-layer polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device featuring a central adipose chamber and an adjacent endothelialized vascular channel, separated by a flexible, porous membrane. The membrane will be functionalized with ECM-derived peptides to support cell adhesion. Using integrated pneumatic actuators, we will apply defined cyclic tensile strain to the adipose chamber. Human adipose-derived stem cells (ASCs) and endothelial cells will be used to form scaffold-free adipose spheroids and vascular networks. Real-time, high-resolution confocal microscopy will be used to quantify cell viability, apoptosis (Caspase-3), vascular network formation (CD31), and macrophage polarization (CD206/CD86) under static, perfused, and dynamically strained conditions, with ECM hydrogel-supported grafts as a control.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, high-fidelity in vitro model for studying fat graft biology under dynamic mechanical loads. It will provide definitive, mechanistic data on the necessity of scaffolds for graft survival, linking mechanical cues to specific immunomodulatory and vascularization pathways. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation, minimally invasive grafting strategies.
proposals.novelty: This approach uniquely integrates dynamic mechanical strain—a critical but often neglected factor in graft failure—with perfusion in a controlled microfluidic system to directly test the scaffold necessity paradigm. It applies Dr. Badylak's expertise in ECM-mediated immunomodulation to a scaffold-free context within a tunable, real-time analysis platform.

proposals.title: Comparative Efficacy of Immediate vs. Delayed Extracellular Matrix Scaffold Implantation for Soft Tissue Reconstruction Following Radiotherapy in a Porcine Model
proposals.motivation: Extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds are widely used for immediate soft tissue reconstruction, but oncologic resections often require adjuvant radiotherapy, which can compromise healing. The prevailing clinical paradigm favors immediate reconstruction, yet this may expose the scaffold to a hostile, irradiated microenvironment, potentially leading to poor integration and complications. A systematic comparison of reconstruction timing in a radiated field is needed to establish evidence-based surgical guidelines.
proposals.hypothesis: Delayed reconstruction with an ECM scaffold, performed after the acute inflammatory phase of radiotherapy has subsided, will yield superior functional and histological outcomes compared to immediate scaffold implantation in a radiated surgical bed.
proposals.method: We will employ a validated porcine model of soft tissue resection and adjuvant radiotherapy. Animals will be randomized into immediate reconstruction (ECM scaffold placed at time of resection, prior to radiotherapy) and delayed reconstruction (resection and radiotherapy followed by scaffold placement 4 weeks later) groups. Outcomes will be assessed at 12 weeks via quantitative histomorphometry for cellular infiltration and neovascularization, biomechanical testing for tensile strength, and multiplex immunoassays to characterize the macrophage polarization (M1/M2) profile within the remodeling scaffold.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct preclinical evidence comparing the timing of ECM-based reconstruction in a radiated field. It will define the immunomodulatory and biomechanical consequences of scaffold placement relative to radiotherapy, offering a scientific basis to challenge or refine the current preference for immediate reconstruction. The findings could directly inform clinical trial design for head and neck or breast cancer patients requiring post-resection radiotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are known to modulate the immune response, their interaction with the specific temporal sequence of radiotherapy and surgery is unexplored. This proposal directly tests the non-obvious strategy that delaying reconstruction to allow radiation-induced inflammation to resolve may paradoxically enhance scaffold-mediated pro-healing outcomes.

proposals.title: Optimizing Breast Reconstruction Volume Retention: A Preclinical Study of Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds Combined with Sub-Defect Volume Fat Grafting
proposals.motivation: Autologous fat grafting (AFG) for post-mastectomy reconstruction often requires overfilling the defect by 20-30% to account for unpredictable volume loss, necessitating multiple procedures and donor site morbidity. The clinical need for reliable single-stage reconstruction with minimal graft volume is significant. Extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds, as pioneered by Badylak's group, have demonstrated pro-regenerative and pro-angiogenic effects, suggesting potential to enhance fat graft survival.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implanting a porous, pro-angiogenic ECM scaffold within a mastectomy defect will create a supportive niche that enables fat grafting at a volume equal to or less than the defect volume to achieve adequate, stable final volume by significantly reducing graft resorption and promoting host tissue integration.
proposals.method: We will utilize a validated rodent model of mastectomy defect. Defects will be reconstructed with: 1) AFG alone at 130% defect volume (clinical standard control), 2) AFG at 100% defect volume, 3) AFG at 70% defect volume, and 4-5) Groups 2 & 3 combined with a surgically implanted, sterilized urinary bladder matrix (UBM-ECM) scaffold pre-seeded with the fat graft. Volumetric analysis via serial MRI at 0, 4, 12, and 24 weeks will quantify retention. Endpoint histology (H&E, CD31) will assess graft architecture, vascularization, and scaffold integration, while cytokine profiling of explanted scaffolds will characterize the local immune microenvironment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first preclinical evidence defining the quantitative relationship between ECM scaffold use and required fat graft volume, moving beyond qualitative assessments of scaffold efficacy. It will establish a foundational protocol for scaffold-enhanced, volume-efficient AFG, directly informing the design of future clinical trials aimed at reducing procedure burden in breast reconstruction.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds are known to improve fat graft retention, their specific capacity to enable grafting at sub-defect volumes—effectively decoupling the initial grafted volume from the final reconstructed volume—has not been systematically tested. This challenges the prevailing surgical axiom that overfilling is always necessary.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of Extracellular Matrix Scaffold Surgical Technique: A Quantitative Analysis of Placement, Configuration, and Fixation on Healing Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds are widely used in regenerative medicine, but clinical outcomes can be variable. A significant, unaddressed source of this variability is the lack of standardized surgical technique for scaffold handling and implantation. Current practice relies on individual surgeon preference, creating a critical gap between scaffold design and reproducible clinical efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable parameters of scaffold surgical technique—including placement geometry, folding method, and fixation strategy—directly influence host cell infiltration, neovascularization, and pro-healing macrophage polarization, thereby determining functional healing outcomes.
proposals.method: Using a well-established rodent model of abdominal wall repair, we will systematically vary three key technical variables: scaffold orientation (subcutaneous vs. intra-muscular placement), physical configuration (flat sheet vs. folded/rolled), and fixation method (suture, fibrin sealant, or self-adhesion). Outcomes will be assessed at 7, 14, and 28 days via histomorphometry for cellular infiltration and angiogenesis, qPCR for M1/M2 macrophage markers (e.g., iNOS, Arg1), and biomechanical testing for tensile strength. A multivariate statistical model will identify the optimal protocol combination.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first evidence-based, quantitative protocol for ECM scaffold implantation, directly linking surgical technique to mechanistic healing responses. It will provide surgeons with a standardized methodology to improve reproducibility and efficacy in clinical applications, from hernia repair to volumetric muscle loss.
proposals.novelty: While prior Badylak lab research has focused on scaffold composition and host immune response, this study uniquely isolates and quantifies the independent effect of *surgical technique* as a critical variable, a factor previously acknowledged but never systematically deconstructed in pre-clinical models.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Spatiotemporal Mapping of Extracellular Matrix Scaffold Degradation and Host Tissue Remodeling in a Rat Volumetric Muscle Loss Model
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds hinges on their degradation profile, which must be synchronized with constructive tissue remodeling. Pre-2026 studies, including Badylak's work on anastomotic healing and immunomodulation, demonstrate that scaffold degradation products are bioactive, but a comprehensive, serial timeline linking scaffold absorption to specific phases of cellular infiltration, vascularization, and tissue maturation is lacking. This gap limits the rational design of next-generation scaffolds with optimized degradation kinetics for specific clinical applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the degradation of a urinary bladder matrix (UBM) scaffold follows a predictable, multi-phase timeline that is spatially and temporally coupled to distinct waves of host immune cell recruitment, vascular ingrowth, and myogenic progenitor cell activation, ultimately leading to functional tissue integration.
proposals.method: We will implant a standardized UBM-ECM scaffold in a rat latissimus dorsi volumetric muscle loss defect. Using a serial sacrifice study design (n=8/time point), tissues will be harvested at 1, 3, 7, 14, 28, 56, and 84 days post-implantation. Histologic analysis (H&E, Masson's Trichrome, immunohistochemistry for macrophages [CD68, CD206], endothelial cells [CD31], and myogenic cells [Pax7, MyoD]) will be performed. Quantitative image analysis will map scaffold area fraction, cellular composition, and vascular density over time. Correlation analysis will define the temporal relationships between scaffold degradation stages and key remodeling events.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, integrated timeline of UBM scaffold fate and host response, directly linking scaffold degradation phases to specific cellular and tissue-level outcomes. It will establish critical benchmarks for 'normal' remodeling, offering a foundational dataset against which the effects of scaffold modifications (e.g., crosslinking) or host comorbidities can be compared to improve clinical predictability.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies report endpoint outcomes or isolated mechanisms, this proposal systematically maps the entire dynamic process, treating scaffold degradation not as a simple loss of mass but as a driver of spatially coordinated biologic events, a concept supported by but not yet fully characterized in pre-2026 work on matrix-bound nanovesicles and degradation products.

proposals.title: Bioreactor-Based Pre-Vascularization of Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds with Adipose-Derived Stem Cells for Enhanced In Vivo Integration
proposals.motivation: The clinical success of extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds in regenerative medicine is often limited by inadequate host vascularization post-implantation, leading to poor cell infiltration and core scaffold necrosis. While pre-seeding scaffolds with cells is a known strategy, static seeding methods are inefficient and fail to mimic the dynamic mechanical and biochemical cues necessary for robust pre-vascular network formation. This project addresses the critical need for a controlled, scalable method to engineer functional microvasculature within ECM scaffolds before surgical placement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that culturing adipose-derived stem cells (ASCs) on a biosynthetic ECM scaffold within a perfusion bioreactor that provides cyclic strain and defined angiogenic gradients will promote the formation of a stable, anastomosis-capable microvascular network within the scaffold prior to implantation, significantly accelerating in vivo perfusion and integration.
proposals.method: We will utilize a decellularized porcine urinary bladder ECM scaffold. Human ASCs will be statically seeded and then transferred to a custom-designed, computer-controlled perfusion bioreactor system. The bioreactor will provide: 1) continuous, low-shear medium perfusion via a peristaltic pump, 2) cyclic mechanical strain (5-10%) through a pneumatic chamber, and 3) controlled delivery of VEGF and FGF-2 via a dual-syringe pump to establish a spatial concentration gradient. Cultures will be maintained for 14 days. Outcome measures will include scaffold cellularity (DNA quantitation), endothelial differentiation (CD31/vWF immunofluorescence), 3D network morphology (confocal microscopy), and functional perfusion capacity in an ex vivo rat aortic ring assay.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a standardized, translatable protocol for creating pre-vascularized ECM constructs, directly addressing a major bottleneck in thick tissue engineering. It will provide fundamental insights into how combined biophysical and biochemical cues within a bioreactor environment direct ASC vasculogenesis. The resulting optimized construct is expected to demonstrate superior survival and integration in subsequent pre-clinical models of soft tissue repair.
proposals.novelty: While bioreactors have been used for tissue engineering, their application is primarily for maturation post-seeding. This proposal innovatively applies bioreactor principles to the *pre-conditioning* phase specifically for ECM scaffolds, integrating cyclic strain with spatially defined morphogen gradients to guide ASC fate—a combination not yet fully explored in pre-2026 literature for pre-vascularization.

proposals.title: Quantitative MRI of Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel-Supported Fat Graft Survival and Integration in a Porcine Soft Tissue Reconstruction Model
proposals.motivation: Autologous fat grafting is limited by unpredictable volume retention due to poor graft survival and integration. Pre-2026 work by Badylak's group demonstrates that extracellular matrix (ECM) hydrogels provide a pro-regenerative scaffold that improves outcomes in anastomosis and modulates healing. A major clinical barrier is the lack of non-invasive, quantitative methods to longitudinally assess graft viability and scaffold remodeling, forcing reliance on terminal histology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ECM hydrogel scaffolds will significantly improve fat graft survival and vascular integration compared to graft-only controls, and that advanced quantitative MRI sequences will correlate strongly with histological measures of adipocyte viability, neovascularization, and scaffold degradation.
proposals.method: We will use a porcine soft tissue defect model with three groups: ECM hydrogel alone, fat graft alone, and fat graft + ECM hydrogel. Serial 3T MRI will be performed at 1, 4, 8, and 12 weeks post-implantation. Sequences will include: 1) Chemical Shift-Encoded MRI for fat fraction mapping to quantify viable adipose volume; 2) Intravoxel Incoherent Motion (IVIM) MRI to separate perfusion (pseudodiffusion) from true tissue diffusivity, assessing microvascularization; and 3) Magnetization Transfer (MT) imaging to probe macromolecular content, sensitive to ECM hydrogel presence and degradation. MRI metrics will be validated against endpoint histology (H&E, CD31, Masson's trichrome) and immunohistochemistry.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a non-invasive MRI protocol for longitudinal assessment of scaffold-enhanced fat grafting, providing critical biomarkers for graft survival and integration. It will deliver definitive pre-clinical evidence on the volumetric and microstructural benefits of ECM hydrogels for soft tissue reconstruction, directly informing clinical trial design. The work will bridge Badylak's ECM scaffold expertise with quantitative imaging, creating a new paradigm for monitoring regenerative therapies.
proposals.novelty: While ECM hydrogels are known to modulate healing, their specific impact on fat graft survival has not been rigorously quantified with non-invasive imaging. The novel application of a multi-parametric MRI protocol (fat fraction, IVIM, MT) to deconvolve adipocyte viability, perfusion, and scaffold remodeling in a single session represents a significant methodological advance over existing, purely volumetric MRI assessments.

proposals.title: Localized Modulation of the Host Response via Anti-Fibrotic and Pro-Angiogenic Coatings on Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds for Soft Tissue Repair
proposals.motivation: Extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds promote constructive remodeling, but clinical outcomes can be suboptimal due to excessive fibrosis or insufficient vascularization. Pre-2026 work demonstrates that ECM degradation products and matrix-bound nanovesicles can modulate the immune microenvironment. However, the localized, sustained delivery of specific immunomodulatory agents from ECM scaffolds to directly steer the healing response remains an underexplored therapeutic strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: Coating a degradable ECM scaffold with localized, eluting anti-fibrotic (e.g., pirfenidone) and pro-angiogenic (e.g., deferoxamine) agents will shift the host macrophage polarization towards a pro-regenerative phenotype, thereby reducing fibrotic encapsulation and enhancing functional vascularization in a rodent model of volumetric muscle loss.
proposals.method: We will fabricate urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-ECM hydrogel scaffolds. Using established orthopedic coating techniques (e.g., layer-by-layer polyelectrolyte deposition or physical adsorption), we will functionalize scaffolds with pirfenidone and deferoxamine. Release kinetics will be characterized in vitro. Scaffolds will be implanted in a rat abdominal wall defect model. Outcomes at 14 and 28 days will include histomorphometry (collagen deposition, capillary density), immunohistochemistry (macrophage phenotypes: CD68, iNOS, CD206), and biomechanical testing of repair site strength.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a methodology for actively programming the host response to ECM scaffolds via drug delivery, moving beyond passive scaffold properties. It will provide direct evidence that co-delivery of anti-fibrotic and pro-angiogenic cues can synergistically enhance the pro-healing immunomodulation intrinsic to ECM biomaterials, leading to more functional tissue restoration.
proposals.novelty: While drug-eluting coatings are common in orthopedics, their application to bioactive, degradable ECM scaffolds to fine-tune the immune-mediated healing process is novel. This approach combines the 'instructive' signals of the ECM with targeted pharmacological modulation, a significant advance over using either strategy alone.

proposals.title: A Biologic Scaffold as a Spatial Guide for Targeted Fat Graft Sculpting and Volume Correction in a Porcine Model
proposals.motivation: Autologous fat grafting is crucial for soft tissue reconstruction, but achieving precise, predictable, and symmetric volume correction remains a significant clinical challenge. Current techniques rely heavily on surgeon skill and lack a biological template to guide the selective removal or contouring of grafted adipose tissue, leading to suboptimal aesthetic outcomes and revision procedures.
proposals.hypothesis: Implantation of a pre-shaped, degradable extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffold will act as a bio-instructive template, guiding host cell infiltration and neotissue formation while providing a stable, three-dimensional boundary that enables targeted, image-guided liposuction for precise volume sculpting.
proposals.method: We will fabricate porous, shape-specific scaffolds from decellularized porcine urinary bladder ECM. These will be implanted subcutaneously in a porcine model, followed by autologous fat grafting. After a 12-week maturation period, we will use pre-operative 3D imaging (MRI) co-registered with intraoperative ultrasound to visualize the scaffold boundary. A robotic-assisted liposuction system, programmed to restrict suction to areas outside the scaffold-defined volume, will be used for targeted fat removal. Histology and micro-CT will assess scaffold integration, neotissue formation, and contour accuracy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, scaffold-guided paradigm for adipose tissue engineering that transitions from passive volume filling to active, precise sculpting. It will provide proof-of-concept for a two-stage, image-guided surgical technique to correct asymmetry and refine contours in soft tissue reconstruction, potentially reducing revision rates.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the traditional use of ECM scaffolds as passive fillers, instead leveraging them as stable, bio-integrated guides for the *subtractive* sculpting of living tissue, a concept not explored in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely integrates Badylak's ECM platform with precision surgical technology for volume control.

proposals.title: A Hybrid Reconstruction Strategy Combining Extracellular Matrix Hydrogel and Autologous Flap Surgery for Enhanced Volume Restoration in Large Abdominal Wall Defects
proposals.motivation: Large, complex soft tissue defects, such as those following oncologic resection or trauma, present a significant reconstructive challenge. While autologous tissue flaps provide vascularized coverage, they are limited by donor site morbidity and often insufficient volume for optimal contour restoration. Pre-2026 work by Badylak and others has demonstrated the pro-healing and immunomodulatory properties of extracellular matrix (ECM)-based scaffolds, but their integration with established flap techniques to address volumetric deficiency remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the co-implantation of a urinary bladder matrix (UBM)-derived hydrogel with a pedicled flap will enhance defect volume filling and improve long-term shape retention by providing a bioactive scaffold that promotes site-appropriate tissue ingrowth and modulates the local immune microenvironment to support flap integration and neovascularization.
proposals.method: In a rat model of a full-thickness abdominal wall defect (3x4 cm), animals will be randomized to receive reconstruction with a pedicled latissimus dorsi flap alone (control) or a flap augmented with an injectable UBM-ECM hydrogel placed at the interface between the flap and the defect bed. The hydrogel will be prepared per established, pre-2026 protocols. Primary outcomes will be volumetric analysis via serial micro-CT imaging at 4, 8, and 12 weeks and biomechanical testing for tensile strength. Secondary outcomes will include histological assessment for cellular infiltration, vascular density (CD31 staining), macrophage polarization (M1/M2 markers), and collagen deposition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first preclinical evidence for a synergistic, hybrid approach to large-volume reconstruction. It will define the mechanistic role of an ECM scaffold in enhancing flap-based repair by quantifying its impact on volumetric maintenance, mechanical integrity, and the host immune response. The findings will establish a translational framework for combining biologic and autologous strategies in complex reconstructive surgery.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds have been studied as standalone fillers or for anastomotic support, their deliberate use as a bioactive volumetric adjunct to a vascularized flap represents a novel combinatorial strategy. This approach moves beyond simple mechanical filling to leverage the documented immunomodulatory cues of the ECM to actively improve the flap microenvironment.

proposals.title: Temporal Optimization of Adipose Regeneration Using Extracellular Matrix Scaffolds: A Protocol to Minimize Fat Grafting Sessions
proposals.motivation: Autologous fat grafting is a cornerstone of soft tissue reconstruction, but its efficacy is limited by unpredictable volume retention and the need for multiple procedures. Pre-2026 research demonstrates that extracellular matrix (ECM) scaffolds provide a pro-regenerative microenvironment, yet the optimal strategy for integrating staged fat grafting with scaffold remodeling to maximize adipose regeneration in a single session remains undefined. This gap prevents the translation of scaffold-based reconstruction into a more efficient, single-stage clinical reality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, precisely timed fat grafting procedure into a pre-conditioned, partially vascularized ECM scaffold will yield superior adipose tissue volume and quality compared to multiple grafting sessions, by synergistically aligning adipogenesis with the scaffold's endogenous immunomodulatory and angiogenic response.
proposals.method: We will utilize a rodent model of subcutaneous defect reconstruction with a clinically relevant, decellularized porcine urinary bladder ECM scaffold. Groups will receive fat grafts at different time points post-scaffold implantation (e.g., day 0, 7, 14, 21), with graft volume standardized. A multi-graft control group will simulate current clinical practice. Outcomes will be assessed at 12 weeks via high-resolution 3D volumetric MRI, histomorphometry for adipocyte area and vascular density, and qPCR for adipogenic (PPARγ, C/EBPα) and pro-healing macrophage (CD206, ARG1) markers. The optimal protocol will be defined by the combination yielding the highest retained volume and mature adipose tissue structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a data-driven, optimized surgical protocol for scaffold-enhanced fat grafting, directly informing clinical trial design. It will provide mechanistic insight into how the dynamic, immune-mediated remodeling phase of an ECM scaffold influences adipocyte progenitor cell survival and differentiation, moving beyond the scaffold as a passive filler to an active biological participant in tissue engineering.
proposals.novelty: While ECM scaffolds and fat grafting have been combined, the novelty lies in systematically defining the critical temporal variable—grafting timing relative to scaffold remodeling—to engineer a single-stage procedure. This integrates the known immunomodulatory properties of ECM (e.g., matrix-bound nanovesicles) with adipose regeneration kinetics in a previously unexplored, clinically pragmatic way.

ideas.title: Standardized Surgical Protocol for Implanting Biofabricated ECM Membranes in Complex Co-culture Tissue Interfaces
ideas.description: This research direction systematically develops and validates a single, optimal surgical protocol for the placement, folding, and fixation of ECM membranes when used to model various complex tissue interfaces (e.g., blood-brain barrier, alveolar-capillary unit, intestinal-vascular interface). By rigorously varying scaffold handling and surgical parameters within a controlled co-culture environment, the goal is to identify the technique that maximizes membrane integrity, cellular interaction, and functional mimicry across different tissue models. This creates a standardized, reproducible platform that not only advances fundamental co-culture research but also directly informs future surgical best practices for implanting biofabricated membranes in regenerative medicine.

ideas.title: A dynamic membrane-based microfluidic scaffold for testing scaffold-free fat graft survival and vascularization
ideas.description: This research fuses organ-on-a-chip membrane technology with the core question of scaffold necessity in fat grafting. It involves designing a multi-layer microfluidic device where a flexible, porous membrane separates a vascular channel from an adipose tissue chamber. The system can dynamically apply cyclic strain (mimicking tissue movement) and perfusion to test whether pre-vascularized adipose spheroids or externally expanded adipocytes can achieve volumetric retention and vascular integration without a permanent scaffold, compared to scaffold-supported controls. This allows for high-resolution, real-time analysis of graft survival mechanisms under physiologically relevant mechanical and biochemical conditions not possible in static cultures or simple animal models.

ideas.title: Primed Scaffold-Directed ECM Integration
ideas.description: The approach first fabricates a 3D microdevice or scaffold with an integrated, non-sacrificial biofunctional priming layer at its intended tissue interface. This priming layer presents precisely engineered biochemical cues that mimic native basement membrane components. Following device fabrication, cells are introduced, and they interact with this primed surface, which actively directs and nucleates the subsequent in situ deposition and remodeling of a natural, cell-secreted ECM. This creates a seamless, stable, and biologically integrated hybrid construct where the synthetic device and living tissue are interlocked through a bioengineered interface.

ideas.title: Correlate Engineered Vascular Architecture with Aesthetic Outcomes in Bioprinted Soft Tissue Constructs
ideas.description: This research direction integrates sacrificial bioprinting of vascular channels with standardized photometric analysis and patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) to establish objective correlations between engineered vascular architecture and the long-term aesthetic quality of bioprinted soft tissue constructs. By creating constructs with systematically varied sacrificial network designs (e.g., channel diameter, spacing, branching), we can quantify how these parameters influence not only cell viability and perfusion but also post-implantation outcomes like skin color match, surface texture, and volume stability through longitudinal 3D photogrammetry and validated PROMs. This approach moves beyond assessing viability alone, creating a closed-loop feedback system where aesthetic success metrics directly inform the computational design of next-generation sacrificial vascular networks. The ultimate goal is to generate predictive models that allow surgeons to select or customize a vascular blueprint optimized for both functional integration and superior aesthetic results in reconstructive surgery.

ideas.title: Fabrication of 3D tissue constructs via layer-by-layer stacking with interfacial bio-fusion of ECM membranes
ideas.description: This approach builds 3D tissues by sequentially stacking suspended ECM hydrogel membranes. Crucially, after each new layer is added, a precisely controlled interfacial treatment is applied—such as a spray of a mild crosslinker or a transient pH change—to partially remodel and fuse the boundary between the new layer and the one beneath it. This creates a continuous, interpenetrating network at the interface while preserving the distinct compositional identity of each layer's core. Cells can be seeded atop each layer before stacking or infiltrated after fusion, enabling them to migrate and form connections across a cohesive, biologically integrated 3D construct.

ideas.title: Employ advanced 3D imaging from materials science
ideas.description: Use micro-CT or synchrotron imaging to create 3D models of scaffold degradation and vascular ingrowth non-destructively.
ideas.sourceTitle: Histomorphologic Outcomes of GalaFLEX Scaffold Used in Breast Surgery: Clinical Follow-up From 6 Weeks to 63 Months.

ideas.title: Single-Cell Encapsulation Fibre Arrays for Deconstructing Complex Tissue Interfaces
ideas.description: This research direction develops sub-micron, tunable ECM fibres to encapsulate individual cells from two or more distinct tissue types (e.g., epithelial and endothelial, neuronal and glial). These single-cell fibres are then assembled into spatially defined, three-dimensional arrays that recreate the architecture of complex native tissue interfaces, such as the blood-brain barrier or the pulmonary alveolus. This platform allows for the high-resolution, mechanistic dissection of paracrine signaling, contact-dependent communication, and ECM-mediated cues across the interface in a fully controlled, modular environment. Consequently, it moves beyond modeling static co-cultures to dynamically engineering and probing the minimal functional units of tissue boundaries.

ideas.title: Organ-on-a-Chip with Dynamically Tunable, Bio-Responsive 'Living' Membranes for Physiologically Adaptive Microenvironments
ideas.description: This research designs next-generation organ-on-a-chip devices centered on a dynamically tunable, 'living' membrane that overcomes the static limitations of traditional materials. The core innovation is a stimuli-responsive hydrogel membrane whose mechanical properties (e.g., stiffness, permeability) and biochemical landscape can be actively and reversibly modulated in real-time to mimic developmental, homeostatic, or disease-related changes in the extracellular matrix. This adaptive control is achieved through integrated microfluidic networks for precise reagent delivery and embedded micro-actuators (optical, electrical) that alter the membrane state, all guided by continuous biosensor feedback from the cultured tissue. The system enables unprecedented modeling of dynamic organ-level processes like fibrosis, wound healing, and metastatic invasion by providing a physiologically relevant, evolving mechanical and biochemical interface.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Multi-Organ-Chip' Strategy to Model Tissue Interfaces on the ECM Membrane
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying an analogy from Microphysiological Systems. The analogous solution from 'multi-organ-chip' development involves a modular, fluidically linked platform for studying interconnected tissues. Adapting this, the research idea is to design a versatile holder system for the ECM membrane, transforming it into a central, customizable interface unit. This adapted platform would enable the establishment of various complex tissue co-cultures (e.g., for alveolar-capillary or blood-brain barrier models) on opposite sides of the membrane, with integrated perfusion controls to model and study cross-tissue signaling, barrier function, and systemic drug transport at diverse tissue interfaces beyond the glomerulus.

ideas.title: Sacrificial Templating via Dissolvable Microfibers for Hierarchical Vascular Networks
ideas.description: This idea adapts a sacrificial templating method from civil engineering, where dissolvable microfibers are used to create porous channels in concrete, to the field of bioprinting. The analogous solution involves embedding sacrificial polymeric elements into a matrix and later removing them to form fluidic networks. Adapted for vascular channel creation, we propose bioprinting with a composite bioink containing sacrificial microfibers of controlled size and solubility. After the surrounding cell-laden hydrogel crosslinks, these microfibers are dissolved via perfusion, yielding immediate, perfusable, and hierarchical vascular lumens embedded within the tissue construct, enhancing nutrient delivery and scalability compared to traditional single-material sacrificial printing.

ideas.title: Pre-vascularized, anastomosable ECM patches with integrated microchannel networks
ideas.description: This approach fabricates large-area ECM patches with an embedded, hierarchical network of endothelial-lined microchannels designed to mimic native vasculature. The channels are created using a sacrificial template technique, resulting in patent lumens that can be surgically anastomosed directly to host arteries and veins. This design ensures immediate perfusion upon implantation, eliminating central ischemia and supporting the survival of thick, cellularized grafts. The system enables the transplantation of metabolically active, organ-specific tissue patches rather than passive acellular membranes.

ideas.title: Modeling dysregulated alveolar-capillary barrier crosstalk in pulmonary fibrosis using a tunable ECM membrane platform
ideas.description: This redesigned study applies the ECM membrane to specifically model the alveolar-capillary interface, co-culturing primary human alveolar epithelial cells and lung microvascular endothelial cells. The core hypothesis is that the membrane's composition and stiffness can be tuned to mimic the fibrotic niche, inducing pathogenic epithelial-mesenchymal crosstalk. The system will quantitatively measure barrier integrity, cytokine profiles, and endothelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EndMT) markers in response to pro-fibrotic stimuli. Success is defined by replicating known hallmarks of fibrotic progression and identifying novel, targetable signaling axes between the cell types.

ideas.title: Enhance membrane stability via polymer network reinforcement inspired by concrete composites
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from civil engineering's approach to enhancing concrete durability, this research seed investigates a composite strategy to improve the long-term stability of biologic membranes in culture. In concrete technology, durability is achieved by embedding a flexible reinforcement network (e.g., polymer fibers) within the brittle matrix to manage stress and prevent crack propagation. Adapted to cell biology, the idea is to develop and integrate a synthetic, biocompatible polymer meshwork that associates with the lipid bilayer, creating a hybrid membrane. This supportive scaffold would provide mechanical reinforcement against the physical and metabolic challenges of extended culture, aiming to improve membrane durability without the potential cytotoxicity or functional disruption of aggressive chemical crosslinking methods.

ideas.title: AI-Guided Acoustic Patterning for Predictive Tissue Model Histology
ideas.description: This research fuses acoustic cell patterning with machine learning-based image analysis to develop intelligent tissue engineering platforms. Acoustic standing waves are used to organize cells into specific, controlled architectures on suspended membranes, creating engineered tissue constructs. A convolutional neural network (CNN) is then trained to analyze high-resolution microscopy images of these constructs, correlating the initial acoustic patterning parameters with the resulting multi-cellular morphology and organization. This learned correlation allows the system to run in reverse: by inputting a desired histological outcome—such as a tissue structure mimicking early-stage ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) or benign hyperplasia—the AI predicts and automatically applies the precise acoustic parameters needed to biofabricate a living tissue model with those targeted features for research or diagnostic training.

ideas.title: Apply Mussel-Inspired Polydopamine Priming for Robust Polyelectrolyte Coating of ECM Fibres
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from marine bioadhesion to solve a potential problem in functionalizing electrospun ECM fibres. In surface chemistry, creating stable polyelectrolyte assemblies on inert or hydrophobic substrates is difficult; this is solved by first applying a mussel-inspired polydopamine (PDA) primer, which adheres strongly to almost any surface and provides a reactive platform for further layering. Here, we prime the ECM fibres with PDA via simple dip-coating in a dopamine solution under mild oxidative conditions. This universal adhesive interlayer ensures robust and uniform attachment of the subsequent layer-by-layer polyelectrolyte film (e.g., chitosan/alginate), leading to a more stable and predictable system for the incorporation and release of therapeutic growth factors or drugs from the fibre scaffold.

ideas.title: Tunable Photodegradable Hydrogels as a Temporal Rheostat for Cell Invasion Studies
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from polymer chemistry's use of photodegradable materials for 4D fabrication, this idea adapts the solution of light-triggered, on-demand degradation. The analogous field uses photodegradable hydrogels with cleavable crosslinkers (e.g., o-nitrobenzyl groups) to dynamically alter material properties with spatial and temporal precision. The adapted research idea is to create ECM-mimetic hydrogels with such photo-labile crosslinks. By applying tunable light patterns, we can degrade the membrane in specific locations at defined times, creating a dynamic 'landscape' of stability. This acts as a temporal rheostat to study how cells probe, sense, and initiate remodeling or invasion programs in direct response to controlled, localized degradation events, providing unprecedented insight into the spatiotemporal logic of cell-ECM feedback loops during processes like invasion and morphogenesis.

ideas.title: Acoustic Patterning of Cells on a Membrane via Nodal/anti-nodal Trapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from optical physics, where optical tweezers and standing wave lattices are used to trap and pattern dielectric particles based on polarizability gradients. The analogous problem of organizing microscopic objects in a field is solved by creating a standing wave pattern that generates force gradients. We adapt this by applying acoustic standing waves to pattern and organize cells on the suspended ECM membrane. In this adapted solution, a standing acoustic wave field is established across the membrane chamber. The acoustic radiation forces generated will drive cells to specific, predictable locations (either pressure nodes or anti-nodes depending on their acoustic contrast), thereby aligning and organizing them into precise, tunable patterns dictated by the acoustic wavelength and chamber geometry.
